questions.stem: What is the Boy Scout Rule as stated in the Clean Code deck?

options.text: You should always leave the code cleaner than you found it
You should never edit code you did not originally write
You should add a comment to every function you touch
You should rewrite any module that lacks unit tests

questions.explanation: The Boy Scout Rule is 'You should always leave the code cleaner than you found it' (CleanCode p.3) - the deck's governing ethic of incremental improvement. Never editing others' code contradicts the call to improve code you pass through; adding comments and rewriting untested modules are not the rule. (CleanCode p.3)

questions.stem: According to the opening slides, what are the 'two reasons' the deck gives for caring about clean code?

options.text: Your manager requires it, and the customer pays for it
You are a programmer, and you want to be a better programmer
It passes code review, and it improves test coverage
It reduces compile time, and it shrinks binary size

questions.explanation: The two reasons are '1. You are a programmer' and '2. You want to be a better programmer' (CleanCode p.2). The other options name external or technical motivations the deck does not use. (CleanCode p.2)

questions.stem: The Boy Scout Rule is best understood as the personal-habit justification for which phase of the change process?

options.text: Concept Location
Impact Analysis
Postfactoring (refactoring after a change)
Initiation

questions.explanation: The guide ties the Boy Scout Rule to Postfactoring: improving messy code you pass through, as a habit rather than a formal phase. Concept Location and Impact Analysis find and assess change; Initiation is the request itself. (CleanCode p.3)

questions.stem: Which set of one-word ideals does the deck attribute to master programmers describing clean code?

options.text: Fast, cheap, scalable
Typed, compiled, optimized
Encrypted, audited, signed
Elegance, literate, care

questions.explanation: The deck lists ideals such as elegance, literate, care, and 'small, expressive, simple' (CleanCode p.4-9). The other options describe runtime or security properties. (CleanCode p.4)

questions.stem: An intention-revealing name should answer which three questions about a variable or function?

options.text: Its type, its size, and its scope
Why it exists, what it does, and how it is used
Its memory address, its lifetime, and its visibility
Who wrote it, when, and in which version

questions.explanation: A name should say why it exists, what it does, and how it is used (CleanCode p.11). Authorship/version belong in VCS; type/size/scope encodings are the disinformation the deck warns against. (CleanCode p.11)

questions.stem: The deck contrasts a meaningless getThem() with a self-documenting method over a gameBoard. What is that method called?

options.text: selectRows()
getActiveItems()
getFlaggedCells()
fetchData()

questions.explanation: The intention-revealing version is getFlaggedCells() over a gameBoard of Cell objects using cell.isFlagged() (CleanCode p.11). The logic equals getThem(); only the names changed. The others are invented. (CleanCode p.11)

questions.stem: Why does the deck call names like lowercase l and uppercase O disinformation?

options.text: They cannot be used as loop counters
They force the compiler to allocate extra memory
They are reserved keywords in most languages
They are visually indistinguishable from the digits 1 and 0

questions.explanation: l and O look like 1 and 0 (int a = l; if (O == l)), misleading the reader (CleanCode p.12). They are not keywords, can be counters syntactically, and have no memory effect. (CleanCode p.12)

questions.stem: In copyChars(char a1[], char a2[]), what fix does the deck recommend to make meaningful distinctions?

options.text: Rename a1/a2 to source and destination
Convert the arrays to a single String
Add a third array argument for the result
Prefix the names with their type, e.g. arrChar1

questions.explanation: Noise-word names a1/a2 carry no information; rename them source and destination (CleanCode p.12). Extra arguments/type changes alter behaviour; type-prefixing is Hungarian notation, an anti-pattern. (CleanCode p.12)

questions.stem: Why does the deck prefer class Customer with generationTimestamp over class DtaRcrd102 with genymdhms?

options.text: Pronounceable names can be spoken aloud and discussed in review
Shorter names compile faster
Abbreviations prevent naming collisions
Encoded names hide implementation details from clients

questions.explanation: If you cannot pronounce it you cannot discuss it; generationTimestamp can be spoken in review, genymdhms cannot (CleanCode p.13). Compile speed and collisions are irrelevant; encoding data into names is discouraged. (CleanCode p.13)

questions.stem: Rewriting for (int j=0; j<34; ...) s += (t[j]*4)/5; with named constants mainly satisfies which naming rule?

options.text: Use pronounceable names
Use searchable names
Avoid mental mapping
Add gratuitous context

questions.explanation: Magic literals 34, 4, 5 cannot be grep'd; naming them (WORK_DAYS_PER_WEEK etc.) makes them searchable (CleanCode p.14). Pronounceability and mental mapping are other rules; gratuitous context is to be avoided. (CleanCode p.14)

questions.stem: The member prefix m_dsc is an example of which naming anti-pattern?

options.text: A searchable constant
An intention-revealing name
An encoding that modern IDEs make redundant
A solution-domain name

questions.explanation: Member prefixes encode the member role; modern IDEs make this redundant, so use 'description' (CleanCode p.15). It is the opposite of intention-revealing, not a constant, not a CS term. (CleanCode p.15)

questions.stem: What is the core problem with Hungarian notation such as PhoneNumber phoneString?

options.text: It makes the variable immutable
It prevents the IDE from autocompleting the name
It forces the field to be public
The name is not changed when the type changes, so it lies

questions.explanation: The deck's note is 'name not changed when type changed!' so phoneString lies once it is no longer a string (CleanCode p.16). It does not affect mutability, autocompletion, or visibility. (CleanCode p.16)

questions.stem: The deck says nested loops using a/b are worse than i/j. Which naming principle does this illustrate?

options.text: Add meaningful context by grouping into a class
Avoid mental mapping - use names the reader already understands
Pick one word per concept
Use solution-domain names from computer science

questions.explanation: i/j are an established loop-counter convention; a/b force the reader to translate them (mental mapping) (CleanCode p.17). The point is reusing known names, so the other principles do not apply. (CleanCode p.17)

questions.stem: According to the deck, class names should be which part of speech and method names which?

options.text: Class names adjectives; method names nouns
Class names verbs; method names nouns
Class names nouns/noun phrases; method names verbs/verb phrases
Both class and method names should be adjectives

questions.explanation: Class names should be nouns/noun phrases and not verbs; method names should be verbs/verb phrases (CleanCode p.18-19). The other options invert or scramble these. (CleanCode p.18)

questions.stem: Which class name does the deck flag as too vague to use?

options.text: Account
WikiPage
AddressParser
Manager

questions.explanation: Vague class names to avoid include Manager, Processor, Data, Info (CleanCode p.18). AddressParser, Account, WikiPage are given as good specific names. (CleanCode p.18)

questions.stem: For overloaded constructors, what does the deck say is generally better than new Complex(23.0)?

options.text: A static factory method like Complex.fromRealNumber(23.0)
A constructor taking a flag argument
A builder object configured with setters
A global factory singleton reached via a registry

questions.explanation: Static factory methods with descriptive names: Complex.fromRealNumber(23.0) is 'generally better than' new Complex(23.0) (CleanCode p.19). Builders, flags, and singletons are different mechanisms. (CleanCode p.19)

questions.stem: Mixing controller, manager, and driver for the same idea across a codebase violates which rule?

options.text: Pick one word per concept
Use searchable names
Avoid disinformation
Use solution-domain names

questions.explanation: Using different words for one concept is 'confusing'; pick one word per concept (CleanCode p.20). It is not about searchability, disinformation, or CS-domain naming. (CleanCode p.20)

questions.stem: Using add for both numeric addition and inserting an item into a list violates which naming rule?

options.text: Pick one word per concept
Don't pun - avoid using the same word for two purposes
Avoid mental mapping
Use pronounceable names

questions.explanation: Don't pun: 'avoid using the same word for two purposes' - add for addition vs list-insertion (CleanCode p.20). 'One word per concept' is the inverse rule; the others are unrelated. (CleanCode p.20)

questions.stem: Why does the deck consider solution-domain names like AccountVisitor or JobQueue acceptable?

options.text: They are shorter than problem-domain names
They avoid the need for any comments
The people who read your code will be programmers, so CS terms carry precise meaning
They encode the variable's type

questions.explanation: Readers are programmers, so terms like Visitor or Queue carry precise CS meaning (CleanCode p.21). Length, comment-avoidance, and type-encoding are not the rationale. (CleanCode p.21)

questions.stem: Bare fields firstName, lastName, street, city, state, zipcode are ambiguous alone. What is the deck's preferred fix?

options.text: Encode their type into each name
Add a journal comment listing them
Make all of them static constants
Group them into a class Address

questions.explanation: Add meaningful context by grouping the fields into a class Address (prefixing like addrState is the lesser alternative) (CleanCode p.21). Type-encoding is Hungarian notation; comments and constants do not help. (CleanCode p.21)

questions.stem: For MAC, port, and web addresses, what does the deck recommend instead of the gratuitous AccountAddress class name?

options.text: Use a single Address class for every address kind
Use precise names like PostalAddress, MAC, or URI
Encode the address family in a flag argument
Always prefix with the owning module name

questions.explanation: Address is a fine class name; over-qualifying with AccountAddress is gratuitous - prefer PostalAddress, MAC, URI for specific kinds (CleanCode p.22). Module-prefixing everything is itself gratuitous context. (CleanCode p.22)

questions.stem: What is the deck's two-part 'rule of functions'?

options.text: They should be public, and documented
They should be fast, and faster than that
They should be small, and smaller than that
They should be generic, and more generic than that

questions.explanation: The rule of functions is '1. should be small / 2. should be smaller than that' (CleanCode p.23). The other phrasings substitute speed, genericity, or visibility for size. (CleanCode p.23)

questions.stem: What concrete size heuristics does the deck give for a function?

options.text: Under 5 lines and under 40 characters per line
Under 50 lines with no character-per-line limit
Under 100 lines and under 80 characters per line
Under 20 lines and under 150 characters per line

questions.explanation: The heuristics are '< 150 characters per line' and '< 20 lines' (CleanCode p.23). The other figures are not the stated thresholds. (CleanCode p.23)

questions.stem: The deck's slogan 'Functions should do one thing. They should do it well. They should do it ___' is completed by which word?

options.text: only
fast
twice
later

questions.explanation: The slogan ends 'THEY SHOULD DO IT ONLY' (CleanCode p.23). The other words contradict the single-responsibility intent. (CleanCode p.23)

questions.stem: The Stepdown Rule says code should read in what order?

options.text: Top to bottom, each function followed by those one level of abstraction below it
Bottom to top, with low-level helpers first
Alphabetically by function name
Grouped with all private methods at the very top

questions.explanation: The Stepdown Rule: code reads top to bottom like a narrative, each function followed by those one level below it (CleanCode p.24). The other orderings are not the rule. (CleanCode p.24)

questions.stem: Blending getHtml(), PathParser.render(pagePath), and .append(newline) in one function violates which rule?

options.text: Command/Query Separation
One level of abstraction per function
Don't Repeat Yourself
Avoid flag arguments

questions.explanation: Those are high, intermediate, and 'remarkably low' abstraction levels mixed in one place - violating one level of abstraction per function (CleanCode p.24). CQS, DRY, and flag arguments concern other smells. (CleanCode p.24)

questions.stem: A switch statement on a type code (e.g. EmployeeType in payAmount) primarily violates which two ideas?

options.text: DRY and LSP
ISP and CQS
SRP and OCP
REP and CCP

questions.explanation: Switching on a type code violates SRP (does many things) and OCP (a new type forces editing it) (CleanCode p.25). DRY/LSP/ISP/CQS and the package principles describe different problems. (CleanCode p.25)

questions.stem: What is the deck's recommended fix for a payAmount() that switches on EmployeeType?

options.text: Convert the switch to a chain of if-else statements
Add a default case that throws an exception
Cache the type code to avoid repeated switching
Push the switch into a factory and replace it with polymorphism

questions.explanation: Hide one switch inside object creation (a factory) and replace the scattered switches with polymorphism - an abstract EmployeeType.payAmount overridden per subclass (CleanCode p.26). if-else chains, throwing, and caching do not remove the violation. (CleanCode p.26)

questions.stem: According to the deck, the ideal number of arguments for a function is which?

options.text: Two (dyadic)
Zero (niladic)
Three (triadic)
One (monadic)

questions.explanation: The deck states 'the ideal number of arguments for a function is zero' (niladic), then one, then two; three should be avoided (CleanCode p.27). Fewer is preferred. (CleanCode p.27)

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of the deck's three legitimate uses of a single (monadic) argument?

options.text: A transformation returned as the value, e.g. StringBuffer transform(StringBuffer in)
Asking a question about it, e.g. boolean fileExists("MyFile")
Mutating a global flag based on the argument's type
Operating on it and returning a transformed result, e.g. InputStream fileOpen("MyFile")

questions.explanation: The three legitimate monadic forms are ask-a-question, operate-and-return, and transformation-as-return-value (CleanCode p.28). Mutating a global based on the argument's type is not one of them. (CleanCode p.28)

questions.stem: Why does the deck prefer StringBuffer transform(StringBuffer in) over void transform(StringBuffer out)?

options.text: Return values are faster than out-parameters
out-parameters violate the Law of Demeter
void functions cannot be unit-tested
The transformation should appear as the return value

questions.explanation: For a transformation, 'the transformation should appear as the return value' (CleanCode p.28). Testability, speed, and the Law of Demeter are not the reasons. (CleanCode p.28)

questions.stem: Why is a boolean flag argument like render(true) considered a code smell?

options.text: It announces the function does more than one thing
It prevents the function from being inlined
Booleans cannot be passed by reference
It forces the caller to allocate an object

questions.explanation: A flag argument signals the function does more than one thing; split render(true) into renderForSuite() and renderForSingleTest() (CleanCode p.29). The other claims are technical non-issues. (CleanCode p.29)

questions.stem: Why does the deck call assertEquals(expected, actual) 'problematic'?

options.text: The argument order is not natural and is easy to get backwards
It takes two arguments instead of one
It returns a value as well as performing an action
It depends on a concrete class

questions.explanation: assertEquals(expected, actual) is problematic because the argument order is not natural (CleanCode p.30); the deck suggests assertExpectedEqualsActual. The issue is order, not arity, CQS, or dependency. (CleanCode p.30)

questions.stem: How does the deck reduce makeCircle(double x, double y, double radius) to fewer arguments?

options.text: Make x and y global variables
Wrap x and y into an argument object: makeCircle(Point center, double radius)
Add a flag argument to choose the coordinate system
Return the circle's centre instead of passing it

questions.explanation: Wrap related arguments into an argument object: makeCircle(Point center, double radius) (CleanCode p.31). Globals, flags, and changing the return value do not address the count cleanly. (CleanCode p.31)

questions.stem: Renaming write(name) to writeField(name) is the deck's example of which technique?

options.text: Command/Query Separation
Extract try/catch blocks
Verbs and keywords - encoding argument meaning into the function name
Replacing a switch with polymorphism

questions.explanation: Adding 'Field' encodes the argument's meaning into the name (CleanCode p.31). The other techniques address error handling, queries vs commands, and type switches. (CleanCode p.31)

questions.stem: The call if (set("username","unclebob")) is ambiguous. Which rule does it violate and what is the fix?

options.text: DRY; extract the duplicated string literals into a constant
Law of Demeter; split the call into local variables
ISP; give each client its own interface
Command/Query Separation; separate the query (attributeExists) from the command (setAttribute)

questions.explanation: set() both sets a value and answers whether it existed - violating CQS; separate attributeExists from setAttribute (CleanCode p.32). DRY, Law of Demeter, and ISP address different smells. (CleanCode p.32)

questions.stem: What does the deck call 'the root of all evil in software'?

options.text: Inheritance
Duplication
Global state
Recursion

questions.explanation: Under DRY the deck says 'duplication may be the root of all evil in software' (CleanCode p.33). Recursion, inheritance, and global state are not given that label. (CleanCode p.33)

questions.stem: What is the deck's nuanced position on Dijkstra's 'one entry, one exit' structured-programming rule?

options.text: It has been entirely superseded by polymorphism
It must be enforced rigidly in every function regardless of size
For small functions an occasional extra return, break, or continue can be more expressive
It applies only to functions that throw exceptions

questions.explanation: For small functions an 'occasional multiple return, break, or continue ... can sometimes even be more expressive' than rigid single-exit (CleanCode p.33). The other options are too absolute or off-topic. (CleanCode p.33)

questions.stem: What does the deck mean by 'do one thing' when applied to functions?

options.text: A function should be called from only one place
A function should return exactly one value
A function should contain exactly one statement
A function should perform a single, well-defined task at one level of abstraction

questions.explanation: Doing one thing means a single well-defined task, reinforced by one level of abstraction per function (CleanCode p.23-24). It is not about statement count, call sites, or return arity. (CleanCode p.24)

questions.stem: The event form void passwordAttemptFailedNtimes(int attempts) is described by the deck as which kind of monadic function?

options.text: One that alters system state from its argument
One that returns a transformed copy of its argument
One that asks a question about its argument
One that should be split because of a flag

questions.explanation: The event form 'alters system state from the argument' (CleanCode p.28). It is distinct from the question and transformation forms, and it carries no flag argument. (CleanCode p.28)

questions.stem: Why does the deck say writeField(name) is easier to understand than writeField(outputStream, name)?

options.text: Fewer arguments (monadic) read more clearly than dyadic where one argument is implied context
outputStream must always be a global
Dyadic functions cannot be overloaded
The second form violates Command/Query Separation

questions.explanation: A monadic call is easier to read than a dyadic one when an argument is incidental context (CleanCode p.30). Globals are not required, dyadic functions can be overloaded, and CQS is unrelated. (CleanCode p.30)

questions.stem: What is the deck's headline rule about comments and bad code?

options.text: Every function must have a Javadoc comment
Comments do not make up for bad code - don't comment bad code, rewrite it
Comments should restate what each line of code does
Comments are forbidden in all production code

questions.explanation: The headline rule is 'Comments do not make up for bad code' - rewrite it instead (CleanCode p.34). Mandated Javadoc and restating code are smells; an outright ban is not the rule. (CleanCode p.34)

questions.stem: Replacing a // Check ... eligible comment plus a flag test with employee.isEligibleForFullBenefits() illustrates which principle?

options.text: Use a journal comment
Add an informative comment
Explain yourself in code
Use a position marker

questions.explanation: Extracting a named method to replace an explanatory comment is 'explain yourself in code' (CleanCode p.34). Journal comments, informative comments, and position markers are different (and the latter two are not what this does). (CleanCode p.34)

questions.stem: Which of these is a GOOD comment category in the deck?

options.text: Journal comment (embedded change log)
Position marker (banner of slashes)
Commented-out code
Legal comment (copyright/license header)

questions.explanation: Legal comments (copyright/license headers) are good (CleanCode p.35). Journal comments, position markers, and commented-out code are all in the bad-comment taxonomy. (CleanCode p.35)

questions.stem: A comment documenting the format a regex matches, e.g. // format matched kk:mm:ss EEE, MMM dd, yyyy, is which good-comment type?

options.text: Warning of consequences
Informative comment
Mandated comment
Explanation of intent

questions.explanation: Documenting a matched format is an informative comment (CleanCode p.35). Intent explains why; warnings flag danger; mandated comments are a bad category. (CleanCode p.35)

questions.stem: A comment saying 'This is our best attempt to get a race condition by creating a large number of threads' is which good-comment type?

options.text: Redundant comment
Clarification
Explanation of intent
Amplification

questions.explanation: Explaining why you did something (the intent) is an explanation-of-intent comment (CleanCode p.36). Clarification translates obscure code; amplification stresses importance; redundant is a bad category. (CleanCode p.36)

questions.stem: assertTrue(a.compareTo(b) == -1); // a < b is which good-comment type?

options.text: Warning of consequences
Mumbling
Amplification
Clarification

questions.explanation: Translating obscure code (compareTo == -1) into readable terms (a < b) is clarification (CleanCode p.36). Amplification stresses importance; warnings flag danger; mumbling is a bad category. (CleanCode p.36)

questions.stem: A comment stressing that a .trim() 'is real important - it removes the starting spaces' is which good-comment type?

options.text: Amplification
Informative comment
Clarification
Noise comment

questions.explanation: Stressing the importance of something easy to overlook is amplification (CleanCode p.37). Clarification translates; informative documents format; noise says nothing. (CleanCode p.37)

questions.stem: Which is among the good comments the deck explicitly endorses for public interfaces?

options.text: Javadocs in public APIs
Javadocs in nonpublic code
Closing-brace comments
Attributions and bylines

questions.explanation: Well-described public-API Javadocs are good - 'nothing quite so helpful' (CleanCode p.37). Javadocs in nonpublic code, closing-brace comments, and bylines are all bad categories. (CleanCode p.52, p.46, p.47)

questions.stem: A catch block whose only comment is // No properties files means all defaults are loaded, forcing you to read other code, is which bad-comment type?

options.text: Redundant comment
Mumbling
Noise comment
Position marker

questions.explanation: A comment so terse you must read elsewhere to understand it is mumbling (CleanCode p.38). Redundant restates code; noise says nothing; a position marker is a banner. (CleanCode p.38)

questions.stem: A Javadoc whose text is longer than the code it documents and adds nothing is which bad-comment type?

options.text: Mumbling
Mandated comment
Redundant comment
Journal comment

questions.explanation: A comment that restates the code and adds nothing is redundant (CleanCode p.39-40). Mumbling is too terse; mandated is policy-forced; journal is an embedded change log. (CleanCode p.39)

questions.stem: Javadoc forced on every function and variable by policy, producing clutter, is which bad-comment type?

options.text: Redundant comment
Noise comment
Scary noise
Mandated comment

questions.explanation: Comments required by policy on everything are mandated comments (CleanCode p.41). Redundant restates code; noise says nothing; scary noise is copy-pasted wrong noise. (CleanCode p.41)

questions.stem: An embedded 'Changes (from 11-Oct-2001) ...' change log in source is which bad-comment type, and where should it live?

options.text: Informative comment; in the README
Journal comment; in version control
Attribution; in the commit author field
Legal comment; in a LICENSE file

questions.explanation: An embedded change log is a journal comment that version control should hold instead (CleanCode p.42). It is not legal/informative, and attributions are a separate smell. (CleanCode p.42)

questions.stem: /** Default constructor. */ and /** The day of the month. */ are examples of which bad-comment type?

options.text: Mumbling
Mandated comment
Noise comment
Redundant comment

questions.explanation: Comments that say nothing are noise comments (CleanCode p.43). Mandated are policy-forced; redundant restate working logic; mumbling is too terse. (CleanCode p.43)

questions.stem: A copy-pasted noise comment that is subtly wrong, e.g. /** The version. */ private String info;, is called what?

options.text: Nonlocal information
Mumbling
Redundant comment
Scary noise

questions.explanation: Copy-pasted noise comments that are subtly wrong are 'scary noise' (CleanCode p.44). Redundant merely restates; nonlocal sits far from its subject; mumbling is too terse. (CleanCode p.44)

questions.stem: Instead of commenting a complex condition, what does the deck recommend?

options.text: Refactor the condition into a named function or variable
Wrap it in a closing-brace comment
Add a TODO above it
Mark it with a position-marker banner

questions.explanation: Don't use a comment when you can use a function or variable - refactor the condition into a named one (CleanCode p.45). TODOs, brace comments, and banners do not clarify the condition. (CleanCode p.45)

questions.stem: Banner comments like // Actions /////////////// are which bad-comment type?

options.text: Position markers
Closing-brace comments
Attributions
Journal comments

questions.explanation: Banner comments are position markers (CleanCode p.46). Closing-brace comments mark block ends; attributions name authors; journals embed change logs. (CleanCode p.46)

questions.stem: What does a } //while closing-brace comment signal, per the deck?

options.text: The loop has an off-by-one error
Your blocks are too big - shorten the function instead
The code is missing a unit test
The team formatting rules are being followed

questions.explanation: A closing-brace comment is a sign blocks are too big; shorten the function rather than annotate the brace (CleanCode p.46). It does not indicate a bug, a missing test, or good formatting. (CleanCode p.46)

questions.stem: /* Added by Rick */ is which bad-comment type, made redundant by what tool?

options.text: Journal comment; the build system
Legal comment; the license scanner
Attribution/byline; version control
Informative comment; the documentation generator

questions.explanation: Author attributions/bylines are redundant because version control records authorship (CleanCode p.47). They are not journal, legal, or informative comments. (CleanCode p.47)

questions.stem: What is the deck's stance on commented-out code?

options.text: Keep it as documentation of past behaviour
Convert it into a TODO comment
Move it to a closing-brace comment
Delete it; version control remembers it

questions.explanation: Dead commented-out code should be deleted because VCS remembers it (CleanCode p.47). Keeping, converting to TODO, or relocating it all preserve the clutter. (CleanCode p.47)

questions.stem: How does the deck describe HTML markup placed inside source comments?

options.text: An acceptable amplification
An abomination
A mandated documentation form
An informative comment

questions.explanation: The deck calls HTML in source comments 'an abomination' (CleanCode p.48). It is neither informative, amplifying, nor mandated good practice. (CleanCode p.48)

questions.stem: A comment documenting a default port value 8082 placed on an unrelated setter is which bad-comment type?

options.text: Noise comment
Too much information
Nonlocal information
Inobvious connection

questions.explanation: A comment describing something far from the code it sits next to is nonlocal information (CleanCode p.49). Too-much-info dumps excess; inobvious connection is unclear relation; noise says nothing. (CleanCode p.49)

questions.stem: Dumping an RFC excerpt into a source comment is which bad-comment type?

options.text: Mandated comment
Scary noise
Nonlocal information
Too much information

questions.explanation: Dumping excessive external detail (an RFC excerpt) is 'too much information' (CleanCode p.50). Nonlocal sits far from its subject; mandated is policy-forced; scary noise is wrong copy-paste. (CleanCode p.50)

questions.stem: A comment whose relationship to the code it annotates is unclear (the '200 bytes for header info' example) is which bad-comment type?

options.text: Inobvious connection
Mumbling
Nonlocal information
Amplification

questions.explanation: A comment whose connection to its code is not clear is an inobvious-connection smell (CleanCode p.51). Nonlocal sits far away; mumbling is too terse; amplification is a good category. (CleanCode p.51)

questions.stem: What does the deck say about function-header comments on short functions?

options.text: Short functions don't need much description - a good name beats a header
Every function needs a full header comment
Headers should list all callers of the function
Headers must be written in Javadoc

questions.explanation: 'Short functions don't need much description' - a well-named small function beats a header comment (CleanCode p.51). The other options demand exactly the clutter the deck discourages. (CleanCode p.51)

questions.stem: What is the deck's view on Javadoc comments in nonpublic (internal) code?

options.text: They are mandatory for every internal method
The extra formality is wasted on internal code
They are the best form of journal comment
They replace the need for unit tests

questions.explanation: The 'extra formality of the javadoc comments' is wasted on nonpublic code (CleanCode p.52). They are not mandatory internally, not journals, and not test substitutes. (CleanCode p.52)

questions.stem: Roughly how does the deck's coverage of bad comments compare to good comments, and what does that imply?

options.text: Equal coverage - comments are neutral
More on good than bad - comments are generally encouraged
About 18 slides on bad vs ~3 on good - the default position is 'explain yourself in code'
Only bad comments are covered - good comments do not exist

questions.explanation: The deck spends ~18 slides on bad comments vs ~3 on good ones (CleanCode p.34-52), making the default 'explain yourself in code'. Good comments do exist (e.g. legal, intent), so the other options are wrong. (CleanCode p.34)

questions.stem: According to the deck, what is the primary purpose of code formatting?

options.text: Compilation speed
Memory efficiency
Source compression
Communication

questions.explanation: The deck states the purpose of formatting is communication - a professional courtesy to readers (CleanCode p.53). It has nothing to do with compile speed, memory, or compression. (CleanCode p.53)

questions.stem: The 'newspaper metaphor' for a source file says you should read it in which direction?

options.text: Right to left, like an Arabic newspaper
High-level concepts at the top, details further down
Randomly, since order does not matter
Low-level details first, summary at the bottom

questions.explanation: The newspaper metaphor: read high-level to details, top of file to bottom (CleanCode p.53). The other directions contradict it. (CleanCode p.53)

questions.stem: What does the deck say each blank line between concepts represents (vertical openness)?

options.text: A marker that a comment was deleted
Wasted space that should be removed
A visual cue identifying a new and separate concept
A required separator between every statement

questions.explanation: 'Each blank line is a visual cue that identifies a new and separate concept' (CleanCode p.53). It is not waste, not required between every statement, and not a deletion marker. (CleanCode p.53)

questions.stem: What does 'vertical density' imply about closely related lines of code?

options.text: Density should be maximised by removing all whitespace
Density measures how many comments a block contains
Related lines should be separated by blank lines
Vertical density implies close association, so related lines belong vertically close

questions.explanation: 'Vertical density implies close association'; related lines should sit close together, and gratuitous comments break that density (CleanCode p.54). The other options invert or misdefine it. (CleanCode p.54)

questions.stem: Per the vertical-distance rule, where should a local variable be declared?

options.text: As close to its usage as possible
In a separate declarations module
At the very top of the file regardless of use
After the function that uses it

questions.explanation: Variables should be declared 'as close to their usage as possible'; instance variables go at the top of the class (CleanCode p.55). The other placements contradict the rule. (CleanCode p.55)

questions.stem: For dependent functions, the vertical-distance rule says they should be placed how?

options.text: Vertically close, with the caller above the callee
Vertically close, with the callee above the caller
In alphabetical order regardless of call relationship
In separate files to reduce coupling

questions.explanation: Dependent functions should be vertically close with the caller above the callee (matching the Stepdown Rule) (CleanCode p.55). Callee-first, alphabetical, and file-splitting all break the reading flow. (CleanCode p.55)

questions.stem: What is the deck's surprising stance on horizontally aligning columns of declarations?

options.text: It is mandatory for all variable declarations
Martin discourages it; ordinary indentation is enough
It must be done only for constants
It is required by most team rules

questions.explanation: Martin actually discourages aligning columns of declarations; ordinary indentation suffices (CleanCode p.57-58). The other options wrongly mandate alignment. (CleanCode p.57)

questions.stem: What does the deck say about collapsing a block onto one line to save space (breaking indentation)?

options.text: Always do it for one-statement blocks
Do it only inside loops
Don't do it - keep the indentation structure
Do it to satisfy the 20-line rule

questions.explanation: Don't collapse blocks onto one line; keep the indentation structure (CleanCode p.59). The other options endorse exactly what the deck warns against. (CleanCode p.59)

questions.stem: When a programmer's personal formatting preferences conflict with the team's, which wins?

options.text: The most senior developer's rules win
Personal preference always wins
Whatever the IDE defaults to wins
The team rules win

questions.explanation: 'If he works in a team then the team rules' - consistency beats personal preference (CleanCode p.60). Seniority, personal preference, and IDE defaults are not the deciding factor. (CleanCode p.60)

questions.stem: Why is an abstract Point exposing getR()/getTheta()/setPolar(...) more abstract than a concrete Point with public double x, y?

options.text: It cannot be subclassed
It hides whether the point is stored in Cartesian or polar form
It avoids the need for a constructor
It uses fewer bytes of memory

questions.explanation: Data abstraction hides implementation: the abstract Point hides whether storage is Cartesian or polar (CleanCode p.61). Memory, subclassing, and constructors are irrelevant to the point. (CleanCode p.61)

questions.stem: Why is a Vehicle exposing getPercentFuelRemaining() more abstract than one exposing getFuelTankCapacityInGallons() plus getGallonsOfGasoline()?

options.text: It makes the class immutable
It returns a smaller number
It hides the implementation detail of how fuel is measured
It removes the need for a fuel sensor

questions.explanation: Exposing a percentage hides the raw gallon-based implementation - data abstraction (CleanCode p.62). The magnitude of the number, the sensor, and immutability are not the reason. (CleanCode p.62)

questions.stem: According to data/object anti-symmetry, objects and data structures support which opposite kinds of change?

options.text: Both ease adding new types equally
Both ease adding new functions equally
OO eases adding new functions; procedural code eases adding new types
OO eases adding new types; procedural/data-structure code eases adding new functions

questions.explanation: Objects hide data and expose behaviour, easing new types; data structures expose data, easing new functions - they are opposites (CleanCode p.63). The other options misstate the anti-symmetry. (CleanCode p.63)

questions.stem: Per data/object anti-symmetry, how does the deck characterise a data structure?

options.text: It exposes its data and has no meaningful functions
It is any class with a constructor
It hides its data behind abstractions and exposes behaviour
It is a class that implements an interface

questions.explanation: Data structures 'expose their data and have no meaningful functions'; objects are the opposite (CleanCode p.63). A constructor or interface does not define a data structure. (CleanCode p.63)

questions.stem: The chain ctxt.getOptions().getScratchDir().getAbsolutePath() is the deck's example of violating which principle?

options.text: The Law of Demeter (a 'train wreck')
The Single Responsibility Principle
Command/Query Separation
The Interface Segregation Principle

questions.explanation: Navigating deep object graphs (a train wreck) violates the Law of Demeter - 'talk only to immediate friends' (CleanCode p.64). SRP, CQS, and ISP describe different smells. (CleanCode p.64)

questions.stem: Why does the deck consider ctxt.options.scratchDir.absolutePath (reaching through exposed fields) the worst Law-of-Demeter form?

options.text: It is slower than calling getters
It couples the caller to the internal structure of everything it traverses
It cannot compile in a typed language
It exposes the caller to thread races

questions.explanation: Reaching through exposed fields couples the method to the internal structure of every object it traverses (CleanCode p.64). Speed, compilation, and thread races are not the cited concern. (CleanCode p.64)

questions.stem: How does a Law-of-Demeter violation specifically raise maintenance cost in change-process terms?

options.text: It increases compile time linearly with chain length
It prevents Concept Location from finding the class
It blows up the estimated impact set - a change to Options ripples to every train-wreck caller
It forces every test to be rewritten as an integration test

questions.explanation: The guide notes Law-of-Demeter violations enlarge the estimated impact set during Impact Analysis: a change to Options ripples to every caller that train-wrecks through it (CleanCode p.64). The other effects are not the cited mechanism. (CleanCode p.64)

questions.stem: What does the deck recommend using instead of returning error codes?

options.text: Global flags
Magic numbers
Boolean out-parameters
Exceptions

questions.explanation: Prefer exceptions to error codes; nested if (x == E_OK) checks bury the happy path (CleanCode p.65-66). Global flags, magic numbers, and out-parameters reintroduce the same problem. (CleanCode p.65)

questions.stem: What is the main problem with returning error codes such as if (deletePage(page) == E_OK) { ... }?

options.text: They cannot represent success
They force deeply nested checks that bury the happy path
They require a try/catch around every call
They are slower than exceptions at runtime

questions.explanation: Error codes force nested if checks that bury the normal flow; exceptions clean this up (CleanCode p.65-66). They are not inherently slower, can represent success (E_OK), and do not require try/catch. (CleanCode p.65)

questions.stem: What does the deck mean by 'extract try/catch blocks'?

options.text: Put the try block inside the catch block
Remove all try/catch and use error codes
The bodies of try and catch should be one-line calls to extracted methods
Inline the catch body into the try body

questions.explanation: Extract the try and catch bodies into named methods so each is a single call, e.g. try { deletePageAndAllReferences(page); } catch (e) { logError(e); } (CleanCode p.67). The other options misdescribe it. (CleanCode p.67)

questions.stem: 'Error handling is one thing' implies which structural rule for a function containing try?

options.text: Each function should contain exactly one catch and no finally
try blocks should be nested at least two deep
try should be the last statement before return
try should be the first word, with nothing after the catch/finally blocks

questions.explanation: 'If the keyword try exists in a function it should be the very first word, and there should be nothing after the catch/finally blocks' (CleanCode p.68). The other rules are invented. (CleanCode p.68)

questions.stem: To 'define the normal flow' and avoid a special-case catch for missing meals, what does the deck recommend?

options.text: Have the DAO return a MealExpenses object that already knows the per-diem default (Special Case pattern)
Return null and null-check at the call site
Catch the exception and log it silently
Add a flag argument to request the default

questions.explanation: The Special Case pattern: the DAO returns a MealExpenses that encodes the per-diem default, so the caller has no special case (CleanCode p.69-70). Silent catches, nulls, and flags reintroduce special handling. (CleanCode p.69)

questions.stem: What does the deck advise returning instead of null from a collection-returning API?

options.text: An empty collection, e.g. Collections.emptyList()
null with a documented contract
A sentinel object equal to null
A boolean indicating emptiness

questions.explanation: Don't return null; return an empty collection like Collections.emptyList() so callers can just iterate (CleanCode p.71-72). The other options still force null/sentinel handling. (CleanCode p.71)

questions.stem: Why does the deck say 'don't pass null' into methods?

options.text: null arguments make the method static
The method either crashes on null or must defensively check and throw
null cannot be passed in object-oriented languages
Passing null doubles the method's argument count

questions.explanation: Passing null forces the method to crash or defend with checks/throws; forbid null entirely (CleanCode p.73). null can be passed, does not affect static-ness, and does not change arity. (CleanCode p.73)

questions.stem: Why does the guide stress that 'don't return null' and 'don't pass null' are TWO separate rules?

options.text: One applies to objects, the other only to primitives
One is a clean-code rule, the other an OO principle
They govern opposite ends of a call; returning an empty collection is not the same as never passing null
They are actually the same rule stated twice

questions.explanation: The guide flags them as two distinct rules: one about a method's return value, the other about its arguments, and returning an empty collection is not equivalent to never passing null (CleanCode p.71-73). They are not the same rule, nor split by primitive/object or by category. (CleanCode p.71)

questions.stem: What does the First Law of TDD state?

options.text: You may write all production code first and add tests later
You may write tests only after the feature is released
You may skip tests for code under 20 lines
You may not write production code until you have written a failing unit test

questions.explanation: The First Law: 'you may not write production code until you have written a failing unit test' (CleanCode p.74). The other options invert or weaken the test-first discipline. (CleanCode p.74)

questions.stem: The Second Law of TDD says you may not write more of a unit test than is sufficient to fail - and adds which clarification?

options.text: A test must always assert at least twice
Not compiling is failing
A test may call production code that does not exist only if stubbed
A compile error does not count as a failure

questions.explanation: The Second Law adds 'not compiling is failing' (CleanCode p.74). The other options contradict it or invent rules. (CleanCode p.74)

questions.stem: The Third Law of TDD limits production code how?

options.text: You may write production code only after all tests pass
You may write as much production code as the design requires
You may not write more production code than is sufficient to pass the currently failing test
You must write all production code before any test passes

questions.explanation: The Third Law: write no more production code than is sufficient to pass the currently failing test (CleanCode p.74). This forces a tight red-green-refactor cycle. (CleanCode p.74)

questions.stem: Taken together, the Three Laws of TDD produce what working rhythm?

options.text: A single big-bang test phase at the end
A design-first, test-last waterfall
A weekly integration cycle
A tight red-green-refactor cycle of seconds

questions.explanation: The Three Laws force a red-green-refactor cycle of seconds (CleanCode p.74). Weekly, big-bang, and test-last rhythms are the opposite of what they create. (CleanCode p.74)

questions.stem: What does the deck say about the importance of test code relative to production code?

options.text: Test code is just as important as production code
Test code matters only for public APIs
Test code can be sloppy because it never ships
Test code should be deleted after the feature passes

questions.explanation: 'Test code is just as important as production code' - dirty tests are worse than no tests (CleanCode p.75). The other options devalue tests, contrary to the deck. (CleanCode p.75)

questions.stem: According to the deck, what is the single most important quality of a clean test?

options.text: Readability
Performance
Coverage percentage
Brevity in lines

questions.explanation: What makes a clean test is 'three things: readability, readability, and readability' (CleanCode p.75). Performance, coverage, and raw brevity are not the stated priority. (CleanCode p.75)

questions.stem: What does the 'one assert per test' guideline aim to achieve?

options.text: Tests run faster by avoiding extra assertions
Tests come to a single conclusion that is quick and easy to understand
Tests never need setup or teardown
Tests can be generated automatically

questions.explanation: One assert per test means each test reaches a single, easily understood conclusion (CleanCode p.76). Speed, setup-free design, and generation are not the goal. (CleanCode p.76)

questions.stem: The 'single concept per test' rule advises what?

options.text: Combine all related concepts into one large test
Use exactly one test class per package
Minimize asserts per concept and test just one concept per test function
Assert once for every line of production code

questions.explanation: Single concept per test: 'minimize the number of asserts per concept and test just one concept per test function' (CleanCode p.76). The other options describe the opposite or unrelated rules. (CleanCode p.76)

questions.stem: What does the F.I.R.S.T. acronym for clean tests stand for?

options.text: Functional, Integrated, Reliable, Secure, Tested
Fast, Isolated, Reusable, Scalable, Typed
Formal, Indexed, Robust, Simple, Traceable
Fast, Independent, Repeatable, Self-validating, Timely

questions.explanation: F.I.R.S.T. = Fast, Independent, Repeatable, Self-validating, Timely (CleanCode p.77). The other expansions are invented. (CleanCode p.77)

questions.stem: In F.I.R.S.T., what does 'Self-validating' mean?

options.text: The test checks its own coverage
The test produces a boolean pass/fail with no manual inspection
The test regenerates itself when it fails
The test validates the production code's documentation

questions.explanation: Self-validating tests yield a pass/fail result automatically, requiring no manual log reading (CleanCode p.77). The other options misread the term. (CleanCode p.77)

questions.stem: Why does the guide call F.I.R.S.T. and the Three Laws the 'micro-foundation' of Verification and CI's self-testing build?

options.text: They make the build slower but more thorough
They eliminate the need for a separate Verification phase
Fast, independent, self-validating tests are exactly what make a CI build trustworthy
They replace integration testing entirely

questions.explanation: The guide ties F.I.R.S.T. tests to CI: fast, independent, self-validating tests are what make a CI build trustworthy (CleanCode p.77). They underpin, not replace, Verification, and do not slow the build. (CleanCode p.77)

questions.stem: In the standard class organization, which order does the deck prescribe?

options.text: Public functions first, then all variables at the bottom
Alphabetical by member name regardless of kind
Private instance variables, then public static constants, then public functions
Public static constants, then private static variables, then private instance variables, then public functions

questions.explanation: The order is public static constants then private static variables then private instance variables then public functions, with each private utility right after its caller (CleanCode p.78). The other orderings are wrong. (CleanCode p.78)

questions.stem: The deck's first two rules for class size are 'they should be small' and what?

options.text: They should be smaller than that
They should be more generic than that
They should be faster than that
They should be public than that

questions.explanation: Like functions, classes 'should be small' and 'should be smaller than that', measured in responsibilities not lines (CleanCode p.79). The other phrasings substitute speed or genericity. (CleanCode p.79)

questions.stem: How does the deck state the Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)?

options.text: A class or module should have one, and only one, reason to change
A class should have one public method
A class should depend on only one other class
A class should be used by only one client

questions.explanation: SRP: 'a class or module should have one, and only one, reason to change' (CleanCode p.79). It is about reasons to change, not method count, dependency count, or client count. (CleanCode p.79)

questions.stem: How is class size best measured according to the deck?

options.text: By the number of lines of code
By responsibilities, not lines of code
By the number of public methods only
By the size of the compiled bytecode

questions.explanation: Classes should be small measured in responsibilities, not lines (CleanCode p.79). Lines, method count, and bytecode size are not the stated measure. (CleanCode p.79)

questions.stem: What does the deck say maintaining cohesion tends to produce?

options.text: A few large classes
More inheritance hierarchies
Many small classes
Fewer interfaces

questions.explanation: 'Maintaining cohesion results in many small classes' - splitting low-cohesion classes yields small focused ones (CleanCode p.79). It does not produce large classes, deeper inheritance, or fewer interfaces. (CleanCode p.79)

questions.stem: What are the four Rules of Simple Design (Emergence), and in what order of priority?

options.text: Expressive, runs all tests, minimal classes, no duplication
No duplication, minimal classes, runs all tests, expressive
Minimal classes, expressive, no duplication, runs all tests
Runs all tests, no duplication, expressive, minimal classes and methods

questions.explanation: Kent Beck's rules in priority order: runs all the tests, no duplication, expressive, minimal classes and methods (CleanCode p.80). The other orderings scramble the priority. (CleanCode p.80)

questions.stem: Which Rule of Simple Design is the highest priority, and why?

options.text: Expressive - readability outranks correctness
Runs all the tests - a design that can't be verified is not done
No duplication - DRY is the supreme rule
Minimal classes - smaller systems are always better

questions.explanation: 'Runs all the tests' is first: a design that cannot be verified is not done (CleanCode p.80). Minimality is last; expressiveness and DRY rank below testability. (CleanCode p.80)

questions.stem: How does the Emergence slide bridge clean code (micro) to good design (macro)?

options.text: It argues that minimizing classes should come before correctness
It proves that architecture must be designed up front in full
Applying small rules relentlessly lets good architecture emerge on its own
It shows that tests are optional once a design is clean

questions.explanation: Emergence says apply small rules relentlessly and architecture takes care of itself - the bridge from micro to macro (CleanCode p.80). It does not endorse big-up-front design, optional tests, or minimization-first. (CleanCode p.80)

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of the four symptoms of rotting design in Martin's chapter?

options.text: Fragility
Immobility
Rigidity
Redundancy

questions.explanation: The four symptoms are rigidity, fragility, immobility, and viscosity (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2-3). Redundancy is not one of them. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2)

questions.stem: Which rotting-design symptom is defined as 'the tendency for software to be difficult to change, even in simple ways, where every change cascades into dependent modules'?

options.text: Rigidity
Immobility
Fragility
Viscosity

questions.explanation: Rigidity is hard-to-change software where changes cascade through dependents, turning a two-day change into a multi-week marathon (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2). The others describe breakage, non-reuse, and bad-shortcut temptation. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2)

questions.stem: Which rotting-design symptom is 'the tendency of software to break in many places, often unrelated, every time it is changed'?

options.text: Fragility
Rigidity
Immobility
Viscosity

questions.explanation: Fragility is breakage in many (often unrelated) places on every change, with breakage probability approaching 1 (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2). Rigidity is cascade-difficulty; immobility is non-reuse; viscosity is shortcut-temptation. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2)

questions.stem: Which rotting-design symptom is 'the inability to reuse software from other projects because of its dependency baggage'?

options.text: Fragility
Immobility
Rigidity
Viscosity

questions.explanation: Immobility is non-reuse: the desirable module has too much dependency baggage, so it is rewritten instead of reused (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.3). The others are about change-difficulty, breakage, and shortcuts. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.3)

questions.stem: Viscosity of design exists when which condition holds?

options.text: The build and source-control tooling are slow
Modules break unexpectedly when changed
The design-preserving way to make a change is harder than the hack
A module cannot be reused elsewhere

questions.explanation: Viscosity of design: 'it is easy to do the wrong thing, but hard to do the right thing' - the hack is easier than the design-preserving change (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.3). Slow tooling is viscosity of environment; the others are fragility and immobility. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.3)

questions.stem: Viscosity of environment refers to what?

options.text: A design that resists the correct change
Modules that break in unrelated places
A package that depends on too many others
Slow builds or slow source control that tempt design-degrading shortcuts

questions.explanation: Viscosity of environment is slow builds/source control tempting shortcuts (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.3). A design that resists the correct change is viscosity of design; the rest describe fragility and coupling. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.3)

questions.stem: All four rotting-design symptoms are traced by Martin to what single root cause?

options.text: Lack of a build server
Improper dependencies between modules
Too few unit tests
Insufficient code comments

questions.explanation: All four symptoms are 'directly or indirectly caused by improper dependencies between the modules'; the cure is dependency management via firewalls (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4). Comments, build servers, and test counts are not the root cause. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4)

questions.stem: What does Martin mean by building 'dependency firewalls'?

options.text: Code reviews that reject new dependencies
Network firewalls that isolate microservices
Boundaries (abstractions) across which dependencies do not propagate
Compiler flags that forbid circular imports

questions.explanation: Dependency firewalls are boundaries across which 'dependencies do not propagate' - the OO principles are those firewalls (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4). They are not network firewalls, compiler flags, or review gates. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4)

questions.stem: Why does the guide warn against treating clean code and these principles as 'mere style/aesthetics'?

options.text: Aesthetics matter more than correctness in maintenance
Formatting alone determines the impact set
Style is subjective so the rules cannot be tested
The macro narrative is about maintenance cost: messy dependencies cause the four rotting symptoms

questions.explanation: The exam-worthy why is maintenance cost: messy dependencies cause rigidity/fragility/immobility/viscosity, and clean code shrinks the impact set (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2-3). Style subjectivity, aesthetics-over-correctness, and formatting-determinism are wrong framings. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2)

questions.stem: How does Martin state the Open/Closed Principle (OCP)?

options.text: A module should be open for extension but closed for modification
A module should be open to all clients and closed to none
A module should be closed for extension but open for modification
A module should never be extended once released

questions.explanation: OCP: 'open for extension but closed for modification' (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4). The other phrasings invert or misstate it. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4)

questions.stem: Which OO principle does Martin call the most important, and what is its enabling mechanism?

options.text: OCP; abstraction (e.g. polymorphism/interfaces)
LSP; design by contract
DIP; dependency injection containers
ISP; multiple inheritance

questions.explanation: OCP is the most important OO principle and its enabling mechanism is abstraction (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4-7). The other pairings name different principles/mechanisms. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4)

questions.stem: The modem LogOn function with if (m.type==hayes) ... else if (...courrier) ... violates OCP because what?

options.text: It uses too many local variables
It must be modified and recompiled for every new modem type
It returns an error code instead of throwing
It depends on an abstract Modem interface

questions.explanation: The if/else chain on modem type must be edited for each new modem - an OCP violation (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.5-6). The fix is to depend on a Modem interface, which the function does NOT do here. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.5)

questions.stem: What is the OCP fix for the modem LogOn if/else chain?

options.text: Replace the if/else with a switch statement
Add a default branch that logs unknown modems
Depend only on a Modem interface (Dial/Send/Recv/Hangup) so new modems need no change to LogOn
Inline each modem's dialing code into LogOn

questions.explanation: Depend on the Modem interface so LogOn calls m.Dial(pno) and new modems require no change to LogOn (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.6-7). Switches, default branches, and inlining keep the OCP violation. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.6)

questions.stem: Besides dynamic polymorphism, what other mechanism does Martin cite for achieving OCP?

options.text: Reflection at runtime
Global configuration files
Conditional compilation with macros
Static polymorphism via templates/generics

questions.explanation: Static polymorphism (templates/generics, e.g. template<typename MODEM> void LogOn) achieves OCP too (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.7). Reflection, config files, and macros are not the cited mechanisms. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.7)

questions.stem: What is the architectural payoff of OCP that Martin highlights?

options.text: You eliminate the need for interfaces
You add new features by only adding new code; if you don't change working code you won't break it
You guarantee zero runtime overhead
You can delete old code without testing

questions.explanation: OCP lets you add features 'by only adding new code' - not changing working code means not breaking it, and even partial compliance helps (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.7). The other claims are not the payoff. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.7)

questions.stem: How are the Clean-Code 'replace switch with polymorphism' rule and the macro OCP related?

options.text: Clean code requires switches that OCP removes
They are unrelated; one is about style, the other about architecture
They are the same idea at different scales - polymorphism over a type abstraction is the OCP mechanism
OCP forbids polymorphism, which clean code requires

questions.explanation: The guide cross-links them: replacing a switch with polymorphism (CleanCode p.25-26) is OCP at architectural scale (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4-6). They are the same idea, not opposed or unrelated. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.5)

questions.stem: How is the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) stated?

options.text: Every class should have at most one subclass
Subclasses must not override any base method
Base classes should be substitutable for their subclasses
Subclasses should be substitutable for their base classes

questions.explanation: LSP: 'subclasses should be substitutable for their base classes' (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.8). The other options invert or invent the rule. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.8)

questions.stem: LSP, coined by Barbara Liskov, is most closely related to which idea by Bertrand Meyer?

options.text: Design by Contract
Dependency injection
Aspect-oriented programming
The visitor pattern

questions.explanation: LSP is related to Meyer's Design by Contract (pre/postconditions) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.8, p.11). AOP, DI, and Visitor are unrelated to its formal statement. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.8)

questions.stem: Why is making Circle inherit from Ellipse the canonical LSP violation?

options.text: Circle overriding SetFoci to keep foci coincident breaks the contract Ellipse.SetFoci(a,b) callers rely on
Circle has fewer fields than Ellipse
Ellipse cannot be instantiated
Circle and Ellipse have different constructors

questions.explanation: Circle's SetFoci override breaks the Ellipse contract: assert(e.GetFocusA()==a) fails for a Circle, so a Circle is not substitutable (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.9-11). Field counts, instantiability, and constructors are not the issue. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.10)

questions.stem: In the Design-by-Contract restatement of LSP, a derived method's preconditions and postconditions must satisfy what?

options.text: Preconditions no weaker, postconditions no stronger than the base method
Preconditions no stronger, postconditions no weaker than the base method
Both preconditions and postconditions strengthened
Both preconditions and postconditions weakened

questions.explanation: A subtype must have preconditions no stronger and postconditions no weaker - 'expect no more, provide no less' (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.11). The other options flip the directions. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.11)

questions.stem: Why does Martin say 'LSP violations are latent OCP violations'?

options.text: LSP and OCP are the same principle with different names
Every OCP violation automatically violates LSP
The if (typeid(e)==typeid(Ellipse)) guard a client adds is itself an OCP violation revisited per new subtype
LSP only applies when OCP is already satisfied

questions.explanation: A client defending against a bad subtype with a typeid check creates an OCP violation that must be revisited for every new subtype (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.11-12). They are linked, not identical, and the implication runs LSP-to-OCP, not the reverse. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12)

questions.stem: What is the deeper lesson of 'Circle is-a Ellipse is true in math but breaks LSP'?

options.text: Mathematical truth always justifies inheritance
Circles should never be modeled in code
Ellipses must be made abstract
Inheritance models behavioral substitutability, not is-a taxonomy

questions.explanation: Inheritance models behavioral substitutability, not taxonomy; the math is-a does not guarantee the contract holds (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.9-11). The other options misread the lesson. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.9)

questions.stem: How is the Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP) stated?

options.text: Invert every inheritance hierarchy
Depend upon abstractions; do not depend upon concretions
Depend only on classes you create yourself
Depend upon concretions; do not depend upon abstractions

questions.explanation: DIP: 'Depend upon Abstractions. Do not depend upon concretions.' (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12). The other options invert or misstate it. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12)

questions.stem: Martin says 'if the OCP states the goal of OO architecture, the DIP states the ___' - which phrase fills the blank?

options.text: final result
main exception
primary mechanism
biggest risk

questions.explanation: 'If the OCP states the goal of OO architecture, the DIP states the primary mechanism' (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12). The other words misrepresent the relationship. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12)

questions.stem: How does OO design 'invert' the dependency direction of a procedural design?

options.text: Abstractions depend on details
Every module depends on every other module
High-level policy depends directly on low-level detail
Details depend on abstractions, instead of high-level policy depending on low-level detail

questions.explanation: Procedural designs point dependencies downward from policy to detail; OO inverts this so details depend on abstractions (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12-13). The other options describe the un-inverted or pathological cases. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12)

questions.stem: Why does Martin call abstractions 'hinge points' in DIP?

options.text: They are where the design can bend or be extended without themselves being modified (OCP)
They are the slowest parts of the runtime
They are where the system fails most often
They are where concrete classes are instantiated

questions.explanation: Abstractions are hinge points where the design flexes/extends without being modified (OCP), because abstract things change less than concrete ones (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.13). They are not failure points, hotspots, or instantiation sites. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.13)

questions.stem: What 'mitigating force' does Martin allow against the strict DIP rule of never depending on concretions?

options.text: Stable concretions like string.h are acceptable to depend upon
All concrete classes are always safe to depend on
Abstractions should be avoided when performance matters
Interfaces should be replaced with concrete singletons

questions.explanation: DIP assumes concrete = volatile, but stable concretions (e.g. string.h) are acceptable to depend on (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.14). The other options over- or under-state the exception. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.14)

questions.stem: Object creation forces you to name a concrete class, seemingly breaking DIP. Which pattern resolves this?

options.text: Observer
Abstract Factory
Adapter
Bridge

questions.explanation: Creating an instance is the unavoidable concrete dependency, solved by the Abstract Factory pattern (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.14, p.32). Observer, Adapter, and Bridge address different problems. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.14)

questions.stem: How is the Interface Segregation Principle (ISP) stated?

options.text: One general-purpose interface is better than many specific ones
Interfaces should never be implemented by more than one class
Many client-specific interfaces are better than one general-purpose interface
Every class should implement every interface

questions.explanation: ISP: 'Many client-specific interfaces are better than one general-purpose interface' (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.14). The other options invert or invent it. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.14)

questions.stem: Why does a single 'fat' interface used by clients A, B, and C couple them together?

options.text: It runs slower than three separate interfaces
It cannot be implemented by a single class
It violates the Law of Demeter
A change made for ClientA forces recompilation of ClientB and ClientC

questions.explanation: A fat interface couples clients: a change for one forces recompilation of the others (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.15). Speed, single-class implementation, and the Law of Demeter are not the issue. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.15)

questions.stem: What is the ISP fix for a fat service interface, and how is it implemented?

options.text: Add versioning to the fat interface
Give each client type its own narrow interface, which the Service multiply inherits/implements
Replace the interface with a concrete base class
Merge all clients into one to remove the coupling

questions.explanation: ISP fix: create a narrow interface per client type (ServiceA, ServiceB, ServiceC), implemented by the Service via multiple inheritance/implementation (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.15-16). The other options do not segregate by client. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.15)

questions.stem: What caution does Martin attach to ISP?

options.text: Interfaces should mirror the database schema
Always create one interface per method
Don't over-segregate into hundreds of tiny interfaces
Never use multiple inheritance

questions.explanation: ISP cautions against over-segregating into 'hundreds of different interfaces' (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.16). One-interface-per-method and the other claims are not the caution. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.16)

questions.stem: What does the Release Reuse Equivalency Principle (REP) state?

options.text: Reusable code must never be released
Releases should contain no reusable code
The granule of release is always one class
The granule of reuse is the granule of release

questions.explanation: REP: 'the granule of reuse is the granule of release' - you can't reuse what isn't released and versioned (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17). The other options misstate it. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17)

questions.stem: What does the Common Closure Principle (CCP) state, and what is it analogous to?

options.text: Classes that change together belong together; it is SRP for packages
All classes belong in one package; it is OCP for packages
Classes that are reused together belong together; it is DIP for packages
Classes should never be grouped; it is ISP for packages

questions.explanation: CCP: 'classes that change together belong together', minimizing packages touched by a change - it is SRP for packages (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17). The other options confuse it with reuse or other principles. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17)

questions.stem: What does the Common Reuse Principle (CRP) state?

options.text: Classes that aren't reused together should not be grouped together
Classes that change together should not be grouped together
Every class should be reusable in isolation
Packages should contain exactly one class

questions.explanation: CRP: 'classes that aren't reused together should not be grouped together', because a dependency on a package is a dependency on everything in it (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17-18). The other options misstate it. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17)

questions.stem: Why does a dependency on a package count as a dependency on everything in it (CRP rationale)?

options.text: Packages are loaded into memory all at once
Clients must revalidate against changes to classes they never use
Each package compiles to a single binary
Package names must be globally unique

questions.explanation: CRP's rationale: a package dependency forces clients to revalidate against changes to classes they never use (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17-18). Memory loading, binaries, and naming are not the reason. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18)

questions.stem: Why can REP, CCP, and CRP not all be maximized simultaneously?

options.text: They require different programming languages
They each apply only to a different lifecycle phase
They are mutually exclusive: REP/CRP favor small packages (reusers), CCP favors large ones (maintainers)
Two of them are actually the same principle

questions.explanation: The three cohesion principles are in tension: REP/CRP serve reusers (small packages), CCP serves maintainers (large packages), so they cannot be satisfied at once (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18). They are distinct and language-agnostic. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18)

questions.stem: How should the package structure evolve over a project's life given the cohesion tension?

options.text: It should be fixed at project start and never change
It should always maximize REP from day one
It should merge all packages into one as it grows
It should jitter: CCP-dominant early, REP/CRP-dominant as it stabilizes

questions.explanation: The structure should jitter over the project's life - CCP-dominant early, REP/CRP-dominant as it stabilizes (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18). A fixed structure or single package contradicts the tension. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18)

questions.stem: What does the Acyclic Dependencies Principle (ADP) state?

options.text: Every package must depend on every other package
The dependencies between packages must not form cycles
Dependencies must form exactly one cycle
Packages may form cycles if documented

questions.explanation: ADP: 'the dependencies between packages must not form cycles' (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18). The other options permit or require cycles. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18)

questions.stem: Why is a single accidental dependency like CommError to GUI dangerous under ADP?

options.text: It forces the GUI to become abstract
It always causes a compile error
It can pull a package's whole transitive closure into its build and test
It doubles the runtime memory usage

questions.explanation: An accidental dependency can pull the whole transitive closure into the build/test, increased 'by an abhorrent amount' (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18-20). It does not necessarily error, double memory, or abstract the GUI. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.20)

questions.stem: Which are the two ways Martin describes for breaking a dependency cycle (ADP)?

options.text: Add a runtime guard, or rename the packages
Increase the build timeout, or disable cyclic checks
Delete one of the packages, or merge all packages into one
Create a new package (extract MessageManager), or apply DIP+ISP to invert a dependency via an interface

questions.explanation: Break cycles by creating a new package (extract MessageManager) or by DIP+ISP - invert a dependency with an interface placed in the using package (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.20-22). The other options do not break the cycle structurally. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.20)

questions.stem: What does the Stable Dependencies Principle (SDP) state?

options.text: Depend in the direction of stability
Never depend on any other package
Depend in the direction of instability
Depend equally on all packages

questions.explanation: SDP: 'Depend in the direction of stability' (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.22). The other options invert or void it. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.22)

questions.stem: What makes a package 'stable' in Martin's sense?

options.text: Many other packages depend on it, so it is hard to change
It has no unit tests
It depends on many other packages
It changes frequently

questions.explanation: Stability is dominated by incoming dependencies: a package many others depend on is stable (hard to change, 'responsible', 'independent') (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.22-24). Tests, outgoing dependencies, and frequent change do not define it. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.22)

questions.stem: What is the stability (instability) metric I in the SDP, and what are Ca and Ce?

options.text: I = Ca/(Ca+Ce), where Ca = outgoing and Ce = incoming coupling
I = Ce/(Ca+Ce), where Ca = afferent (incoming) and Ce = efferent (outgoing) coupling
I = Ce/Ca, where Ca and Ce are abstract and concrete class counts
I = Na/Nc, the ratio of abstract to total classes

questions.explanation: I = Ce/(Ca+Ce); Ca is afferent (incoming) coupling and Ce is efferent (outgoing) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23-24). The second swaps the definitions; the others are wrong formulas (Na/Nc is abstractness). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23)

questions.stem: On the instability scale, what do I=0 and I=1 mean?

options.text: I=0 is maximally instable; I=1 is maximally stable
I=0 means abstract; I=1 means concrete
I=0 is maximally stable; I=1 is maximally instable
Both extremes are forbidden

questions.explanation: I=0 is maximally stable, I=1 maximally instable; SDP says depend on packages whose I is lower than yours (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23-24). The second inverts it; abstractness is a different metric. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24)
