questions.stem: A package has Ca=2 incoming and Ce=8 outgoing dependencies. What is its instability I, and is it stable or instable?

options.text: I = 0.2, stable
I = 0.25, instable
I = 4.0, stable
I = 0.8, instable

questions.explanation: I = Ce/(Ca+Ce) = 8/(2+8) = 0.8, which is close to 1, so the package is instable (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23-24). 0.2 would use Ca in the numerator; I cannot exceed 1. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23)

questions.stem: Why does the guide warn against believing 'stable = good, instable = bad'?

options.text: Instable packages cannot be tested
Instability is desirable for packages you want to keep changeable; SDP only forbids stable depending on instable
Stability has no effect on maintenance
Stable packages always contain bugs

questions.explanation: Instability is desirable for packages meant to change; SDP just forbids a stable package depending on an instable one (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24). The other options misjudge stability. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24)

questions.stem: What does the Stable Abstractions Principle (SAP) state?

options.text: All packages should be equally abstract
Stable packages should be concrete packages
Stable packages should be abstract packages
Abstract packages should be instable

questions.explanation: SAP: 'stable packages should be abstract packages' (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24). The other options invert or void it. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24)

questions.stem: How does SAP resolve the SDP dilemma that stable packages are hard to change?

options.text: It removes all dependencies on stable packages
It forbids stable packages entirely
It makes stable packages instable instead
If a stable package is also abstract, it is hard to change but easy to extend (OCP)

questions.explanation: SAP makes stable packages abstract so they are hard to change but easy to extend via OCP - it is a restatement of DIP at package scale (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24-25). The other options misresolve the dilemma. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24)

questions.stem: What is the abstractness metric A, and what range does it take?

options.text: A = Na/Nc (abstract classes / total classes), in [0,1]
A = Ce/(Ca+Ce), in [0,1]
A = Nc/Na (total / abstract classes), unbounded
A = Ca - Ce, any integer

questions.explanation: A = Na/Nc, abstract classes divided by total classes, range [0,1] (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.25-26). Ce/(Ca+Ce) is instability; the others are wrong. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.25)

questions.stem: A package has 3 abstract classes out of 12 total. What is its abstractness A?
questions.explanation: A = Na/Nc = 3/12 = 0.25 (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.25-26). 4.0 inverts the ratio; 0.75 is the concrete fraction; 9 is the concrete count. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.25)

questions.stem: On the A-vs-I graph, what is the 'Main Sequence'?

options.text: The line A = I
The ideal line A + I = 1
The line A + I = 0
The point A = 1, I = 1

questions.explanation: The Main Sequence is the ideal line A + I = 1 - abstract-and-stable (upper-left) or concrete-and-instable (lower-right) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.26-27). The other formulas describe different lines/points. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.26)

questions.stem: The 'Zone of Pain' on the A-vs-I graph is which corner, and why is it bad?

options.text: High-A, high-I: abstract but nobody depends on it
High-A, low-I: abstract and stable, the ideal
Low-A, low-I: concrete and heavily depended-upon, so it can't be extended and is painful to change
Low-A, high-I: concrete and instable, the ideal

questions.explanation: The Zone of Pain is lower-left (A approx 0, I approx 0): concrete and heavily depended-upon, hard to extend and change (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27). High-A/high-I is the Zone of Uselessness; the others are on the Main Sequence. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27)

questions.stem: The 'Zone of Uselessness' is which corner of the A-vs-I graph?

options.text: Low-A, low-I: concrete and depended-upon
Low-A, high-I: concrete and instable
High-A, low-I: abstract and stable
High-A, high-I: abstract but nothing depends on it

questions.explanation: The Zone of Uselessness is upper-right (A approx 1, I approx 1): abstract but unused (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27). Low-A/low-I is the Zone of Pain; the other two are on the Main Sequence. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27)

questions.stem: The distance metric D' = |A + I - 1| measures what, and what does D'=0 mean?

options.text: The abstractness of a package; D'=0 means fully abstract
How far a package strays from the Main Sequence; D'=0 means it sits on it
The number of cyclic dependencies; D'=0 means one cycle
The instability of a package; D'=0 means maximally stable

questions.explanation: D' = |A + I - 1| measures distance from the Main Sequence (0 = on it, 1 = farthest) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27). It is not instability, abstractness, or a cycle count. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27)

questions.stem: How does the deck define a design pattern?

options.text: A unit test that verifies architecture
A brand-new invention specific to one project
A well-worn, known-good solution to a common design problem
A diagram generated automatically by an IDE

questions.explanation: Design patterns are 'repeating structures of design ... a well-worn and known good solution to a common problem' - old techniques proven over years (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28). They are not new inventions, diagrams, or tests. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28)

questions.stem: Which book does the deck point readers to for fuller coverage of patterns?

options.text: Rajlich's Software Engineering: The Current Practice
Beck's Test-Driven Development
Fowler's Refactoring
The Gang of Four book (Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides)

questions.explanation: The deck cites the Gang of Four (GHJV94) book for the rest of the patterns (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28). Fowler, Rajlich, and Beck are referenced elsewhere for different topics. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28)

questions.stem: What are the three categories of the GoF pattern taxonomy?

options.text: Creational, structural, behavioral
Frontend, backend, database
Static, dynamic, hybrid
Cohesion, coupling, stability

questions.explanation: The GoF taxonomy is creational / structural / behavioral (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28). The other groupings are not the GoF categories. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28)

questions.stem: What problem does the Abstract Server pattern solve?

options.text: A client depending directly on a concrete server violates DIP and can't swap servers
A server needs to notify many clients of an event
Two abstractions need to vary independently
Object creation forces a concrete dependency

questions.explanation: Abstract Server addresses a client depending directly on a server (DIP violation): server changes propagate and the client can't swap servers (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28). Notification is Observer; independent variation is Bridge; creation is Abstract Factory. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28)

questions.stem: What is the structure of the Abstract Server pattern?

options.text: Wrap the server in an Adapter that delegates to it
Insert an abstract interface (a 'hinge point') between the Consumer and the concrete ResourceManager
Register Observers with a Subject that notifies them
Quarantine creation in a single factory

questions.explanation: Abstract Server inserts an abstract interface (ResourceManager_I) between Consumer and concrete server - a hinge point on which the design flexes (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28). The other structures describe Adapter, Observer, and Abstract Factory. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28)

questions.stem: Abstract Server is the structural realization of which principle(s)?

options.text: ISP - segregate fat interfaces
REP - granule of reuse equals release
OCP/DIP - program to an interface
SDP - depend toward stability

questions.explanation: Abstract Server realizes OCP/DIP by programming to an interface; GoF files the idea under interface-programming rather than one named pattern (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28). ISP, REP, and SDP address different concerns. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28)

questions.stem: When should you use the Adapter pattern instead of just inserting an abstract interface (Abstract Server)?

options.text: When the server needs to notify many observers
When you must quarantine object creation
When two abstractions must vary independently
When the server is third-party or too heavily depended-upon to change

questions.explanation: Adapter is used when inserting the interface directly is infeasible - the server is third-party or too depended-upon to modify (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29). The other cases are Observer, Abstract Factory, and Bridge. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29)

questions.stem: What is the structure of the Adapter pattern?

options.text: A factory returns abstract products hiding concrete classes
An Adapter implements the abstract interface and delegates each method to the existing concrete server
An abstraction bridges to a separate implementation interface
A Subject holds Observers and calls Update on each

questions.explanation: The Adapter implements ResourceManager_I and delegates: 'every method of the adapter simply translates and then delegates' (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29). The others describe Observer, Abstract Factory, and Bridge. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29)

questions.stem: In the GoF taxonomy, the Adapter pattern is classified as which kind?

options.text: Architectural
Behavioral
Structural
Creational

questions.explanation: Adapter is a GoF structural pattern (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29). It is not behavioral, creational, or 'architectural' (not a GoF category). (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29)

questions.stem: What problem does the Observer pattern solve?

options.text: Object creation forces a concrete dependency
A third-party server cannot be modified
A client can't be allowed to depend on a concrete server
One element must act when another detects an event, but we don't want the detector to know the actor

questions.explanation: Observer: one element acts when another detects an event without the detector knowing the actor - e.g. a Meter updates when a Sensor changes (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29). The others describe Abstract Server, Abstract Factory, and Adapter. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29)

questions.stem: In the Observer structure, which class does Sensor derive from and which interface does Meter implement?

options.text: Sensor derives from Subject; Meter implements Observer (Update())
Both derive from Subject
Sensor implements Observer; Meter derives from Subject
Both implement Adapter

questions.explanation: Sensor derives from Subject (holding a list of Observers via Register); Meter implements the Observer interface (Update()) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30). The other pairings reverse or confuse the roles. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30)

questions.stem: What is the correct sequence of Observer dynamics when the Sensor reading changes?

options.text: Sensor detects change, calls Notify on Subject, which cycles registered Observers calling Update
Meter polls the Sensor on a timer and reads the value
Subject queries each Observer for its state before updating
The Observer creates a new Sensor and copies its value

questions.explanation: Dynamics: Sensor detects change, calls Notify, Subject cycles all Observers calling Update, each Meter reads and displays the new value (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29-30). Polling, reverse-querying, and re-creation are not the flow. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29)

questions.stem: In the GoF taxonomy, the Observer pattern is classified as which kind?

options.text: Structural
Behavioral
Creational
Concurrency

questions.explanation: Observer is a GoF behavioral pattern (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29-30). It is not structural, creational, or a 'concurrency' category. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29)

questions.stem: What is the key decoupling that Observer achieves?

options.text: The consumer creates the source it observes
The source and consumer share one concrete class
The event source (Subject) does not know its event consumers (Observers)
The consumer adapts a third-party interface

questions.explanation: Observer decouples event source from consumer: the Subject doesn't know the Observers, it just Notifies (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29-30). The other options describe creation, merging, or Adapter. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29)

questions.stem: What is the intent of the Bridge pattern?

options.text: Let a Subject notify many Observers of events
Quarantine concrete-class creation in one place
Insert an interface between a client and a server
Decouple an abstraction from its implementation so the two can vary independently

questions.explanation: Bridge decouples an abstraction from its implementation so each varies independently (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30-31). The others describe Observer, Abstract Factory, and Abstract Server. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30)

questions.stem: In the MusicSynthesizer example, what problem motivates the Bridge pattern?

options.text: Creating a synthesizer forces a concrete dependency
EmitVoice is inextricably bound to PlayMidi via inheritance, so neither can be reused or varied alone
A third-party MIDI library cannot be changed
The synthesizer must notify meters when a note plays

questions.explanation: Implementing PlayMidi with inheritance binds EmitVoice so tightly that you can't reuse EmitVoice or give PlayMidi a different voice (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.31). The others describe Observer, Abstract Factory, and Adapter. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.31)

questions.stem: What does the Bridge structure introduce to fix the MusicSynthesizer coupling?

options.text: A global factory that creates synthesizers
A Subject/Observer registration list
A strong separation: MusicSynthesizer (abstract PlayMidi) bridges to a VoiceEmitter interface
An Adapter that delegates to a legacy voice engine

questions.explanation: Bridge introduces a separation between interface and implementation - MusicSynthesizer bridges to a VoiceEmitter interface so EmitVoice and PlayMidi are decoupled and each varies independently (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.31). The others are different patterns. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.31)

questions.stem: In the GoF taxonomy, the Bridge pattern is classified as which kind?

options.text: Creational
Functional
Behavioral
Structural

questions.explanation: Bridge is a GoF structural pattern (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30-31). It is not behavioral, creational, or 'functional'. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30)

questions.stem: What problem does the Abstract Factory pattern solve?

options.text: Creating an instance forces a concrete dependency, violating DIP
A Subject must notify Observers of an event
A client depends directly on a concrete server
An abstraction is too tightly bound to its implementation

questions.explanation: Abstract Factory addresses that creating an instance forces a concrete dependency against DIP; it confines that dependency to one place (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32). The others describe Abstract Server, Observer, and Bridge. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32)

questions.stem: In the modem Abstract Factory example, where is the knowledge of concrete Hayes/Courrier/Ernie classes confined?

options.text: To ModemFactory_I, which is itself only known to main
To every module that creates a modem
To the Modem interface
To a global Observer list

questions.explanation: Only ModemFactory_I knows the concrete modem classes, and only main knows ModemFactory_I - concrete knowledge is quarantined at the top (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32). The other options spread or misplace that knowledge. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32)

questions.stem: In the Abstract Factory modem example, how do clients obtain a modem?

options.text: By calling new Hayes() directly
Through a ModemFactory interface reached via a global GtheFactory, calling Make(string) which returns a Modem*
By registering as an Observer of the factory
By adapting a third-party modem class

questions.explanation: Clients call Make(string) on the ModemFactory interface via the global GtheFactory, getting back a Modem* (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32). Direct new, Observer registration, and adapting are not how it works. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32)

questions.stem: In the GoF taxonomy, the Abstract Factory pattern is classified as which kind?

options.text: Structural
Behavioral
Creational
Relational

questions.explanation: Abstract Factory is a GoF creational pattern (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32). It is not structural, behavioral, or 'relational'. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32)

questions.stem: Why is Abstract Factory described as the resolution to a tension within DIP itself?

options.text: DIP requires factories for all classes by definition
Abstract Factory replaces DIP entirely
DIP and Abstract Factory address unrelated problems
DIP forbids depending on concretions, yet creation must name one; the factory isolates that one concrete dependency

questions.explanation: DIP says don't depend on concretions, but creating an instance forces exactly that; Abstract Factory 'allows that dependency upon the concrete class to exist in one, and only one, place' (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32). It complements, not replaces, DIP. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32)

questions.stem: Why is JHotDraw described as a textbook showcase of GoF design patterns?

options.text: It contains no design patterns, serving as a negative example
Erich Gamma, a GoF author, co-wrote it to demonstrate patterns in a real framework
It was generated automatically from UML diagrams
It was the first program ever written in Java

questions.explanation: JHotDraw is famous because GoF author Erich Gamma co-wrote it to demonstrate patterns in a real framework (guide JHotDraw Connection). The other claims are false. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28)

questions.stem: Which pattern matches JHotDraw's Figure/Drawing model notifying DrawingView and FigureListeners when a figure changes?

options.text: Bridge
Adapter
Observer
Abstract Factory

questions.explanation: Figure-notifies-views is the Observer pattern: the figure (Subject) does not know the views (Observers), it just notifies (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29-30). Adapter, Abstract Factory, and Bridge serve other roles in JHotDraw. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29)

questions.stem: JHotDraw creating tools and figures through factory abstractions (never hard-coding concrete figure classes) corresponds to which pattern?

options.text: Adapter
Bridge
Observer
Abstract Factory

questions.explanation: Creating figures via factory abstractions to avoid hard-coding concrete classes is the Abstract Factory quarantine of concrete-class knowledge (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32). The others are different patterns. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32)

questions.stem: JHotDraw wrapping AWT/Swing components and external types to fit its own interfaces is an example of which pattern?

options.text: Adapter
Abstract Factory
Observer
Bridge

questions.explanation: Adapting AWT/Swing components and external types to JHotDraw's interfaces is the Adapter pattern (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29). Observer, Abstract Factory, and Bridge address other concerns. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29)

questions.stem: Why does adding a new Figure subclass to JHotDraw without editing the framework illustrate OCP?

options.text: The framework is open for extension (new subclasses) but closed for modification (no edits)
It proves the framework has no abstractions
It shows the framework violates the Law of Demeter
It demonstrates Hungarian notation in practice

questions.explanation: Adding a Figure subclass without editing the framework is exactly the OCP payoff: open for extension, closed for modification (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4). The other options are unrelated or false. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4)

questions.stem: How do JHotDraw's clean-code and OO principles keep the change-process impact set small?

options.text: The framework caches every change so impact analysis is skipped
Interface-based design and small SRP classes mean dependencies target abstractions, so changes don't cascade
It uses one giant class, so there is only ever one thing to change
It forbids subclassing, eliminating dependencies

questions.explanation: Interface-based design (DIP) and small single-responsibility classes mean dependencies target abstractions, so Concept Location and Impact Analysis stay tractable and changes don't cascade (guide; DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12, CleanCode p.79). The other options are false. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12)

questions.stem: A JHotDraw that violated the Law of Demeter, returned nulls, and switched on type codes would exhibit which symptoms?

options.text: Improved cohesion and lower coupling
Better reuse across projects
Rigidity and fragility, making every maintenance task expensive
Faster Concept Location

questions.explanation: Such violations would produce the rigidity/fragility symptoms that make maintenance expensive (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2-3). They would worsen, not improve, cohesion, reuse, and Concept Location. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2)

questions.stem: In the worked example, Impact Analysis finds the payAmount switch is duplicated everywhere. This large impact set is a textbook example of which symptom?

options.text: Fragility
Immobility
Viscosity
Rigidity

questions.explanation: A change whose impact set is large because the switch is duplicated and each must be modified is rigidity (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2, p.6). Fragility is unexpected breakage; immobility is non-reuse; viscosity is shortcut temptation. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2)

questions.stem: In the worked example, after Prefactoring with OCP+polymorphism, why does adding CONTRACTOR become cheap?

options.text: The change is deferred until the next release
Actualization is only new code (a new subclass) with no edits to working code, shrinking the impact set to one class
The type code is cached so no edits are needed
The compiler regenerates all the switches automatically

questions.explanation: After OCP refactoring, adding CONTRACTOR is only a new Contractor subclass with no edits to working code, so the impact set is one class (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.7). The other explanations are false. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.7)

questions.stem: In the worked example's Postfactoring step, which clean-code fixes are applied to the payAmount fragment?

options.text: Return null and add defensive null checks
Add a journal comment and align the columns
Express intent in code (isEligibleForFullBenefits), drop m_ encodings, replace the -1 error code
Introduce a flag argument and a closing-brace comment

questions.explanation: Postfactoring applies the Boy Scout Rule: express intent in code, drop m_ encodings, and replace the -1 error code with an exception/special case (CleanCode p.34, p.15, p.65). The other options apply smells, not fixes. (CleanCode p.34)

questions.stem: What is the worked example's central 'moral' about why the CONTRACTOR change is cheap?

options.text: The change is cheap because the team is experienced
The change is cheap because the build server is fast
Clean code is purely about visual aesthetics
Clean code is lower maintenance cost, measured in impact-set size and ease of verification

questions.explanation: The moral: the change is cheap because the code obeys clean-code rules and OO principles - clean code is lower maintenance cost, measured in impact-set size and verification ease (CleanCode p.80; DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.7). It is not aesthetics, team experience, or build speed. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.7)

questions.stem: What does the term 'intention-revealing name' mean?

options.text: A name that says why it exists, what it does, and how it is used
A name reserved by the language standard
A name that encodes the variable's type
A name shorter than three characters

questions.explanation: An intention-revealing name says why it exists, what it does, and how it is used (CleanCode p.11). Type-encoding, reservation, and length are not the definition. (CleanCode p.11)

questions.stem: What is 'disinformation' in naming?

options.text: A name that misleads, such as l/O confusable with 1/0
A name that reveals too much about implementation
A name copied from another codebase
A name with too many syllables

questions.explanation: Disinformation is a name that misleads, e.g. l/O looking like 1/0 (CleanCode p.12). The other options describe different (or non-) problems. (CleanCode p.12)

questions.stem: What is a niladic function?

options.text: A function that takes one argument
A function that takes zero arguments
A function that returns void
A function that throws no exceptions

questions.explanation: Niladic means zero arguments (monadic=1, dyadic=2, triadic=3) (CleanCode p.27). Return type and exception behaviour do not define it. (CleanCode p.27)

questions.stem: What is a monadic function?

options.text: A function that takes zero arguments
A function that takes two arguments
A function that takes exactly one argument
A function with one return statement

questions.explanation: Monadic means one argument (CleanCode p.27-28). Zero is niladic, two is dyadic; return-statement count is unrelated. (CleanCode p.27)

questions.stem: What is a dyadic function?

options.text: A function that takes three arguments
A function that takes one argument
A function defined in two files
A function that takes two arguments

questions.explanation: Dyadic means two arguments (CleanCode p.30). Three is triadic, one is monadic; file count is irrelevant. (CleanCode p.30)

questions.stem: What does DRY stand for?

options.text: Document Reusable Yields
Don't Repeat Yourself
Delay Risky Yields
Define, Refactor, Verify

questions.explanation: DRY = Don't Repeat Yourself; 'duplication may be the root of all evil' (CleanCode p.33). The other expansions are invented. (CleanCode p.33)

questions.stem: What does Command/Query Separation require?

options.text: Queries must change system state
Commands and queries must use separate databases
A function should either do something or answer something, not both
Every command must return a value

questions.explanation: CQS: a function does something OR answers something, not both (CleanCode p.32). The other options describe CQRS-style architecture or contradict the rule. (CleanCode p.32)

questions.stem: What is a flag argument?

options.text: A required first parameter in every method
An argument that names the calling thread
A parameter marking a deprecated function
A boolean parameter signalling the function does more than one thing

questions.explanation: A flag argument is a boolean parameter signalling the function does more than one thing (CleanCode p.29). The other options invent meanings. (CleanCode p.29)

questions.stem: What is the Law of Demeter sometimes summarized as?

options.text: Talk only to your immediate friends
Open for extension, closed for modification
Depend on abstractions, not concretions
One reason to change per class

questions.explanation: The Law of Demeter says 'talk only to your immediate friends', avoiding train wrecks (CleanCode p.64). The other phrases are DIP, OCP, and SRP. (CleanCode p.64)

questions.stem: What is the 'Special Case pattern' used for in error handling?

options.text: Returning an object that encodes the special case so the normal flow stays clean
Catching every exception and ignoring it
Returning null for missing data
Logging errors to a file

questions.explanation: The Special Case pattern returns an object encoding the special case so the caller has no special handling (CleanCode p.69-70). The other options reintroduce special handling or nulls. (CleanCode p.69)

questions.stem: What is the Stepdown Rule about?

options.text: Reducing the number of function arguments step by step
Code reading top to bottom, each function above those one level lower
Stepping through code in a debugger
Lowering indentation by one level each line

questions.explanation: The Stepdown Rule: code reads top to bottom, each function above those one level of abstraction lower (CleanCode p.24). The other options misread it. (CleanCode p.24)

questions.stem: What does 'one level of abstraction per function' forbid?

options.text: Using more than one local variable
Calling other functions
Mixing high, intermediate, and low abstraction in a single function
Returning a value

questions.explanation: It forbids mixing high/intermediate/low abstraction in one function (CleanCode p.24). Local variables, calls, and returns are fine. (CleanCode p.24)

questions.stem: What does the newspaper metaphor describe?

options.text: Printing code documentation as a newspaper
Writing comments in headline style
Formatting code into columns like a newspaper
Reading a file from high-level concepts down to details

questions.explanation: The newspaper metaphor is reading high-level to details, top to bottom (CleanCode p.53). It is not about printing, headlines, or columns. (CleanCode p.53)

questions.stem: What is 'Hungarian notation'?

options.text: Prefixing every name with the class name
Encoding the type into a variable name, e.g. phoneString
Using all-uppercase constant names
Writing variable names in a foreign language

questions.explanation: Hungarian notation encodes the type into the name (phoneString) - an anti-pattern (CleanCode p.16). The other options describe different conventions. (CleanCode p.16)

questions.stem: What does 'mental mapping' refer to as a naming smell?

options.text: Mapping interfaces to implementations
Drawing a diagram of the class hierarchy
Forcing readers to translate cryptic names into the concepts they mean
Memorizing the call stack

questions.explanation: Mental mapping forces the reader to translate cryptic names into concepts (e.g. a/b loop counters) (CleanCode p.17). The other options are unrelated. (CleanCode p.17)

questions.stem: Which is the correct accessor/mutator/predicate naming convention the deck cites?

options.text: read / write / check
load / store / test
fetch / put / has
get / set / is

questions.explanation: The deck uses the get/set/is convention (getName, setName, isPosted) (CleanCode p.19). The other prefix sets are not the cited convention. (CleanCode p.19)

questions.stem: What does 'explain yourself in code' advise instead of a comment?

options.text: Extract a well-named function or variable that makes the comment unnecessary
Add a Javadoc block
Write a longer, clearer comment
Move the comment to a separate file

questions.explanation: Explain yourself in code: replace the comment with a self-naming method like isEligibleForFullBenefits() (CleanCode p.34). Longer comments, Javadoc, and external files do not 'explain in code'. (CleanCode p.34)

questions.stem: What is data abstraction?

options.text: Hiding implementation behind an abstract interface
Removing all data from a class
Storing data only in a database
Encrypting a class's fields

questions.explanation: Data abstraction hides implementation behind an interface (Cartesian vs polar Point) (CleanCode p.61). It is not data removal, database storage, or encryption. (CleanCode p.61)

questions.stem: What does the deck mean by 'searchable names'?

options.text: Names indexed by a search engine
Names you can grep for, replacing magic literals with named constants
Names that appear in the documentation
Names sorted alphabetically

questions.explanation: Searchable names replace magic literals (34, 4, 5) with named constants you can grep (CleanCode p.14). The other options are unrelated. (CleanCode p.14)

questions.stem: What does the deck say about commented-out code with respect to version control?

options.text: Keep it as a backup since VCS is unreliable
Comment it more thoroughly
Delete it - version control remembers it
Move it into the commit message

questions.explanation: Delete commented-out code because VCS remembers it (CleanCode p.47). Keeping or annotating it preserves clutter; commit messages are not the place for it. (CleanCode p.47)

questions.stem: What is a 'journal comment'?

options.text: A copyright header
A comment explaining intent
A TODO note
An embedded change log in the source that VCS should hold instead

questions.explanation: A journal comment is an embedded change log; version control should hold it instead (CleanCode p.42). Copyright headers, intent, and TODOs are different (and some are good). (CleanCode p.42)

questions.stem: What does a TODO comment represent in the deck's good-comment list?

options.text: A banner separating sections
A note flagging work still to be done
An author byline
A redundant restatement of code

questions.explanation: TODO comments are a good category - notes of work to be done (CleanCode p.37). Redundancy, banners, and bylines are bad categories. (CleanCode p.37)

questions.stem: Which principle is abbreviated OCP?

options.text: Ordered Closure Principle
Object Coupling Principle
Open/Closed Principle
Optimal Cohesion Principle

questions.explanation: OCP is the Open/Closed Principle: open for extension, closed for modification (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4). The other expansions are invented. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4)

questions.stem: Which principle is abbreviated LSP?

options.text: Loose Stability Principle
Local Scope Principle
Layered System Principle
Liskov Substitution Principle

questions.explanation: LSP is the Liskov Substitution Principle: subtypes substitutable for base classes (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.8). The others are invented. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.8)

questions.stem: Which principle is abbreviated DIP?

options.text: Dependency Inversion Principle
Direct Injection Principle
Data Integrity Principle
Distributed Interface Principle

questions.explanation: DIP is the Dependency Inversion Principle: depend on abstractions, not concretions (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12). The others are invented. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12)

questions.stem: Which principle is abbreviated ISP?

options.text: Interface Segregation Principle
Internal State Principle
Inverted Stability Principle
Independent Service Principle

questions.explanation: ISP is the Interface Segregation Principle: many client-specific interfaces beat one fat one (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.14). The others are invented. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.14)

questions.stem: Which principle is abbreviated SRP?

options.text: Stable Reuse Principle
Single Responsibility Principle
Static Resolution Principle
Shared Resource Principle

questions.explanation: SRP is the Single Responsibility Principle: one and only one reason to change (CleanCode p.79). The others are invented. (CleanCode p.79)

questions.stem: What does the acronym REP stand for among the package cohesion principles?

options.text: Reusable Encapsulation Principle
Refactor Early Principle
Release Reuse Equivalency Principle
Reduced Effort Principle

questions.explanation: REP is the Release Reuse Equivalency Principle: the granule of reuse is the granule of release (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17). The others are invented. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17)

questions.stem: What does the acronym CCP stand for among the package cohesion principles?

options.text: Cohesive Class Principle
Concrete Coupling Principle
Central Control Principle
Common Closure Principle

questions.explanation: CCP is the Common Closure Principle: classes that change together belong together (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17). The others are invented. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17)

questions.stem: What does the acronym CRP stand for among the package cohesion principles?

options.text: Closed Refactoring Principle
Common Reuse Principle
Client Recompilation Principle
Cyclic Reduction Principle

questions.explanation: CRP is the Common Reuse Principle: classes not reused together shouldn't be grouped together (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17-18). The others are invented. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17)

questions.stem: What does the acronym ADP stand for among the package coupling principles?

options.text: Aggregated Dependency Principle
Abstract Design Principle
Acyclic Dependencies Principle
Adaptive Decoupling Principle

questions.explanation: ADP is the Acyclic Dependencies Principle: package dependencies must not form cycles (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18). The others are invented. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18)

questions.stem: What does the acronym SDP stand for among the package coupling principles?

options.text: Shared Data Principle
Sequential Design Principle
Static Definition Principle
Stable Dependencies Principle

questions.explanation: SDP is the Stable Dependencies Principle: depend in the direction of stability (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.22). The others are invented. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.22)

questions.stem: What does the acronym SAP stand for among the package coupling principles?

options.text: Stable Abstractions Principle
Structured Abstraction Pattern
Single Access Principle
Service Adapter Principle

questions.explanation: SAP is the Stable Abstractions Principle: stable packages should be abstract (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24). The others are invented. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24)

questions.stem: In the coupling metrics, what does Ca stand for?

options.text: Afferent (incoming) coupling
Efferent (outgoing) coupling
Abstract class count
Concrete class count

questions.explanation: Ca is afferent (incoming) coupling; Ce is efferent (outgoing) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23-24). Class counts are Na/Nc in the abstractness metric. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23)

questions.stem: In the coupling metrics, what does Ce stand for?

options.text: Afferent (incoming) coupling
Efferent (outgoing) coupling
Class extension count
Cyclic edge count

questions.explanation: Ce is efferent (outgoing) coupling; Ca is afferent (incoming) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23-24). The other expansions are invented. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23)

questions.stem: What is the formula for instability I?

options.text: I = Ca / (Ca + Ce)
I = Na / Nc
I = Ce / (Ca + Ce)
I = A + 1

questions.explanation: Instability I = Ce/(Ca+Ce) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23). Ca/(Ca+Ce) would invert it; Na/Nc is abstractness. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23)

questions.stem: What is the formula for abstractness A?

options.text: A = Nc / Na
A = Ce / (Ca + Ce)
A = 1 - I
A = Na / Nc

questions.explanation: Abstractness A = Na/Nc (abstract classes / total classes) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.25). Ce/(Ca+Ce) is instability; A=1-I is only true exactly on the Main Sequence. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.25)

questions.stem: What is the equation of the Main Sequence?

options.text: A * I = 1
A + I = 1
A + I = 0
A - I = 0

questions.explanation: The Main Sequence is the line A + I = 1 (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.26). The other equations describe different lines. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.26)

questions.stem: What is the distance-from-Main-Sequence metric?

options.text: D' = |Ca - Ce|
D' = A + I

questions.explanation: The distance metric is D' = |A + I - 1| (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27). The other formulas are not the metric. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27)

questions.stem: What does Design by Contract use to formalize the LSP?

options.text: UML class diagrams
Interface segregation
Unit tests and mocks
Preconditions and postconditions

questions.explanation: Design by Contract formalizes LSP with pre/postconditions (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.11). Tests, diagrams, and ISP are different concepts. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.11)

questions.stem: The Abstract Factory pattern belongs to which GoF category?

options.text: Creational
Behavioral
Structural
Concurrency

questions.explanation: Abstract Factory is creational (it governs object creation) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32). It is not structural, behavioral, or 'concurrency'. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32)

questions.stem: The Observer pattern belongs to which GoF category?

options.text: Behavioral
Structural
Creational
Architectural

questions.explanation: Observer is behavioral (it governs how objects interact via notification) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29). The others are wrong categories. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29)

questions.stem: The Adapter and Bridge patterns both belong to which GoF category?

options.text: Behavioral
Structural
Creational
Functional

questions.explanation: Adapter and Bridge are both structural patterns (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29-31). They are not behavioral, creational, or 'functional'. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29)

questions.stem: Which pattern decouples an abstraction from its implementation so both can vary independently?

options.text: Adapter
Observer
Bridge
Abstract Factory

questions.explanation: Bridge decouples abstraction from implementation so each varies independently (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30-31). The others address delegation, notification, and creation. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30)

questions.stem: Which pattern lets a Subject notify registered Observers without knowing them?

options.text: Adapter
Bridge
Abstract Factory
Observer

questions.explanation: Observer lets a Subject notify Observers without knowing them (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29-30). The others are unrelated to notification. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29)

questions.stem: Which pattern quarantines knowledge of concrete classes in one creation point?

options.text: Adapter
Abstract Factory
Bridge
Observer

questions.explanation: Abstract Factory confines concrete-class creation knowledge to one place (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32). The others do not govern creation. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32)

questions.stem: Which pattern wraps an unchangeable third-party server behind an abstract interface by delegation?

options.text: Abstract Factory
Bridge
Adapter
Observer

questions.explanation: Adapter implements an abstract interface and delegates to an unchangeable server (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29). The others serve different purposes. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29)

questions.stem: A developer says 'making a stable package depend on an instable one is fine as long as both are well tested.' What is wrong?

options.text: Stability has no relationship to dependency direction
Only abstract packages may have dependencies
Nothing; testing removes all coupling concerns
SDP requires depending toward stability - a stable package must not depend on an instable one

questions.explanation: SDP says depend in the direction of stability; a stable package depending on an instable one is exactly the violation, regardless of testing (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.22-24). The other claims contradict SDP. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.22)

questions.stem: A team claims it has maximized REP, CCP, and CRP all at once. Why is this claim suspect?

options.text: The three cohesion principles are in tension and cannot all be satisfied simultaneously
They apply to classes, not packages
Only two of the three are real principles
Maximizing any one automatically maximizes the others

questions.explanation: REP/CCP/CRP are 'mutually exclusive ... cannot simultaneously be satisfied' (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18). All three are real package-level principles, and maximizing one trades off the others. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18)

questions.stem: How do you distinguish Abstract Server from Adapter?

options.text: Abstract Server inserts an interface when you can change the server; Adapter is used when you cannot
Abstract Server is creational; Adapter is behavioral
Abstract Server notifies observers; Adapter creates objects
They are two names for the same pattern

questions.explanation: Abstract Server inserts an interface when the server is changeable; Adapter is for when the server is third-party/too-depended-upon to change (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28-29). The other distinctions are wrong. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28)

questions.stem: How does Bridge differ from Adapter?

options.text: Bridge creates objects; Adapter notifies observers
Bridge separates an abstraction from its implementation up front so both vary; Adapter retrofits an interface onto an existing server
Bridge is behavioral; Adapter is creational
They solve the same problem identically

questions.explanation: Bridge is designed up front to let abstraction and implementation vary independently; Adapter retrofits an interface onto an unchangeable existing server (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.29-31). The other contrasts are wrong. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30)

questions.stem: Why is the deck's Command/Query Separation NOT the same as the architectural CQRS pattern?

options.text: CQS and CQRS are identical and interchangeable
CQS applies only to databases
CQS is the small rule that a function does or answers, not both; CQRS is a larger architectural split
CQRS forbids any query methods

questions.explanation: The deck's CQS is the small rule (a function does or answers, not both), distinct from the architectural CQRS pattern (CleanCode p.32). They are not identical, DB-only, or query-forbidding. (CleanCode p.32)

questions.stem: Is a switch statement always a clean-code violation?

options.text: Yes - all switch statements must be removed
Yes - switches are forbidden by the OCP
No - switches are always preferable to polymorphism
No - one switch hidden inside a factory creating polymorphic objects is acceptable; scattered switches on a type code are the smell

questions.explanation: One switch hidden inside a creating factory is acceptable; scattered type-code switches are the smell (CleanCode p.26; DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.6). Switches are neither always banned nor always preferred. (CleanCode p.26)

questions.stem: Which pair of rotting-design symptoms is most often confused, and how do they differ?

options.text: Rigidity vs immobility, which both mean non-reuse
Rigidity (hard to change, cascades) vs fragility (breaks unexpectedly in unrelated places)
Fragility vs viscosity, which both mean slow builds
Immobility vs viscosity, which are identical

questions.explanation: Rigidity is hard-to-change-with-cascades; fragility is unexpected breakage in unrelated places - the guide warns not to swap them (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2). The other pairings conflate distinct symptoms. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.2)

questions.stem: A subtype tightens a base method's precondition (accepts fewer inputs). Does this satisfy LSP?

options.text: No - because postconditions must be weakened
Yes - tighter preconditions are always safe
No - preconditions must be no stronger than the base's
Yes - only postconditions matter for LSP

questions.explanation: LSP requires preconditions no stronger than the base's; tightening a precondition breaks substitutability (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.11). Both pre- and postconditions matter; postconditions must be no weaker. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.11)

questions.stem: A subtype weakens a base method's postcondition (promises less). Does this satisfy LSP?

options.text: Yes - only preconditions matter for LSP
No - because preconditions must be strengthened
Yes - weaker postconditions are always safe
No - postconditions must be no weaker than the base's

questions.explanation: LSP requires postconditions no weaker than the base's; weakening one breaks the contract callers rely on (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.11). Preconditions must be no stronger, not strengthened. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.11)

questions.stem: A reviewer wants to add a Javadoc to every private helper. What does the deck advise?

options.text: The extra formality of Javadoc is wasted on nonpublic code
Javadoc is mandatory only for private methods
All methods, public or private, require Javadoc
Replace the helpers with comments instead

questions.explanation: Javadoc in nonpublic code is wasted formality (CleanCode p.52); it shines on public APIs (CleanCode p.37). It is not mandatory everywhere or private-only. (CleanCode p.52)

questions.stem: A function begins with setup code, then a try block, then more code after the catch. What rule does this break?

options.text: Error handling is one thing - try should be first and nothing should follow catch/finally
DRY - the setup is duplicated
The Law of Demeter - it talks to strangers
ISP - the function serves too many clients

questions.explanation: 'Error handling is one thing': if try exists it should be the first word with nothing after catch/finally (CleanCode p.68). DRY, Law of Demeter, and ISP describe other problems. (CleanCode p.68)

questions.stem: An API returns null for an empty result and every caller null-checks. What is the clean-code fix?

options.text: Document the null in the Javadoc
Return an empty collection so callers can iterate without null checks
Throw a NullPointerException instead
Return a boolean alongside the null

questions.explanation: Don't return null; return an empty collection (Collections.emptyList()) so callers iterate freely (CleanCode p.71-72). Documenting, throwing NPE, or pairing a boolean do not remove the null discipline problem. (CleanCode p.71)

questions.stem: Which of these is the correct way to make a clean test 'Independent' (the I in FIRST)?

options.text: Each test depends on a shared global fixture
Tests must run in a fixed sequence
Each test runs without depending on the order or outcome of other tests
Each test calls the previous test's setup

questions.explanation: Independent means a test does not depend on other tests' order or outcome (CleanCode p.77). Shared globals, fixed sequences, and chained setup violate independence. (CleanCode p.77)

questions.stem: Which scenario correctly applies the 'Timely' (T in FIRST) property?

options.text: Writing all tests months after release
Writing tests only when a bug is reported
Writing tests in a separate timed sprint
Writing the unit test just before the production code it covers

questions.explanation: Timely means writing the test just before the production code (matching the Three Laws of TDD) (CleanCode p.74, p.77). Late, bug-driven, or separately-timed tests are not timely. (CleanCode p.77)

questions.stem: A package in the Zone of Pain is concrete and heavily depended-upon. What practical trouble does this cause?

options.text: It is abstract and therefore over-engineered
It can't be extended and is painful to change, yet many packages rely on it
It is instable and changes too often
Nobody depends on it, so it is dead code

questions.explanation: The Zone of Pain (low A, low I) is concrete and heavily depended-upon: hard to extend, painful to change (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27). 'Nobody depends on it' is the Zone of Uselessness; it is concrete, not abstract or instable. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27)

questions.stem: Why is a package in the Zone of Uselessness problematic?

options.text: It forms a dependency cycle
It is concrete and over-depended-upon
It is abstract but nothing depends on it, so the abstraction is wasted
It changes on every build

questions.explanation: The Zone of Uselessness (high A, high I) is abstract but unused, wasting the abstraction (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27). Over-dependence is the Zone of Pain; cycles are an ADP issue. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27)

questions.stem: To break a cycle using DIP+ISP, where should the inverting interface be placed?

options.text: In a brand-new third-party library
In the global namespace only
In the package that implements it
In the package that uses (depends on) it

questions.explanation: DIP+ISP breaks a cycle by adding an interface placed in the package that uses it, inverting the dependency (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.20-22). Placing it in the implementer or elsewhere does not invert the dependency. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.20)

questions.stem: A method navigates accountManager.getAccount().getOwner().getAddress().getCity(). What refactoring does the Law of Demeter suggest?

options.text: Avoid the chain - it couples the caller to every intermediate object's structure
Convert the getters to public fields
Cache getCity() in a static field
Add a flag argument to skip levels

questions.explanation: A deep getter chain is a train wreck violating the Law of Demeter - it couples the caller to every traversed object's structure (CleanCode p.64). Caching, public fields, and flags do not address the coupling. (CleanCode p.64)

questions.stem: Which comment is genuinely good rather than a smell?

options.text: A warning that a test takes a long time so don't run it casually
A banner of slashes labeling a section
A } //for at the end of a loop
A /* Added by Sam */ byline

questions.explanation: A warning of consequences (e.g. a slow test) is a good comment (CleanCode p.37). Banners (position markers), closing-brace comments, and bylines are all smells. (CleanCode p.37)

questions.stem: A function named isProcessed() also writes a log entry and updates a counter. Which two rules does this violate?

options.text: DRY and the Law of Demeter
Do one thing and Command/Query Separation
ISP and SDP
OCP and LSP

questions.explanation: A query named isProcessed() that also mutates state violates 'do one thing' (CleanCode p.23) and Command/Query Separation (CleanCode p.32). DRY, Law of Demeter, and the OO/package principles are different. (CleanCode p.32)

questions.stem: Both the Clean-Code switch example and the macro OCP modem example point to one underlying cause of high maintenance cost. What is it?

options.text: Excessive use of interfaces slowing the build
Too many small functions to navigate
Scattered conditionals on a type code that must each be edited when a new type appears
Over-reliance on unit tests

questions.explanation: Both examples show scattered type-code conditionals that must each be modified for a new type - the rigidity-causing pattern OCP/polymorphism removes (CleanCode p.25-26; DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.5-6). The other options are not the cause. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.6)

questions.stem: SAP is described as 'just a restatement of the DIP' at which scale?

options.text: At method scale - methods should be abstract
At statement scale - statements should be polymorphic
At project scale - projects should be reusable
At package scale - stable (depended-upon) packages should be abstract, mirroring 'depend on abstractions'

questions.explanation: SAP restates DIP at package scale: stable, depended-upon packages should be abstract, so dependencies target abstractions (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24-25). It is not a method-, statement-, or project-scale rule. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.24)

questions.stem: CCP is characterized as the package-level analogue of which class-level principle, and why?

options.text: LSP - both concern substitutability
SRP - both gather things that change for the same reason into one unit
ISP - both segregate interfaces
OCP - both forbid modification

questions.explanation: CCP (classes that change together belong together) is SRP for packages: both group things that change for the same reason (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17). OCP, LSP, and ISP address different concerns. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.17)

questions.stem: A package has Ca=0 and Ce=5. What is its instability, and what does this imply under SDP?

options.text: Undefined; division by zero
I=0 (maximally stable); other packages should depend on it
I=1 (maximally instable); it should depend only on more stable packages, never the reverse
I=0.5; it sits on the Main Sequence

questions.explanation: I = Ce/(Ca+Ce) = 5/(0+5) = 1, maximally instable; under SDP it may depend on more stable packages but nothing should depend on it (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23-24). It is not stable, mid-range, or undefined. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.23)

questions.stem: A package sits at A=0.9, I=0.9. Where is it on the A-vs-I graph and what is its distance D'?

options.text: On the Main Sequence; D' = 0.0
Zone of Uselessness; D' = 0.0
Zone of Pain; D' = 0.0
Zone of Uselessness; D' = |0.9+0.9-1| = 0.8

questions.explanation: High A and high I is the Zone of Uselessness; D' = |A+I-1| = |0.8| = 0.8, far from the Main Sequence (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27). The Zone of Pain is low/low, and D' is not zero here. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.27)

questions.stem: A client depends on a concrete logging server you control. Later you must also support a third-party logger you cannot modify. Which two patterns, in order, address these two situations?

options.text: Abstract Server for the controllable server, then Adapter for the third-party one
Observer for both
Adapter first, then Abstract Server
Abstract Factory then Bridge

questions.explanation: Abstract Server inserts an interface when you can change the server; Adapter handles the one you cannot (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28-29). Observer, Abstract Factory, and Bridge do not fit these situations. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28)

questions.stem: You must create modems polymorphically without any high-level module knowing the concrete classes. Which pattern, and which DIP problem does it solve?

options.text: Abstract Factory; it isolates the unavoidable concrete dependency of object creation to one place
Observer; it decouples event source from consumer
Bridge; it separates abstraction from implementation
Adapter; it wraps an unchangeable server

questions.explanation: Abstract Factory isolates the concrete dependency that object creation forces, the one place DIP cannot otherwise avoid (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.14, p.32). The others solve notification, abstraction-implementation split, and legacy wrapping. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.32)

questions.stem: Why does the guide say clean code reduces the cost of the Impact Analysis phase specifically?

options.text: It makes the build server faster
Fewer surprise dependencies mean a smaller estimated impact set
It removes the need for Concept Location
It eliminates the Verification phase

questions.explanation: Clean code and OO principles reduce surprise dependencies, shrinking the estimated impact set that Impact Analysis must compute (guide Overview; DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4). It does not speed builds, remove Concept Location, or eliminate Verification. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4)

questions.stem: Why does the guide call clean code 'the how to write the code well companion' to the rest of the course?

options.text: It replaces the eight-step change process entirely
It applies only during the Conclusion phase
It is orthogonal to the linear change process, focusing on what makes the code being edited good
It is a lab exercise on JHotDraw

questions.explanation: Lecture 6 steps out of the linear change process and asks an orthogonal question: what makes code good and keeps a system changeable (guide Overview). It does not replace the process, confine to Conclusion, or constitute a lab. (CleanCode p.2)

questions.stem: How does the Emergence priority ordering resolve a conflict between 'minimal classes' and 'runs all the tests'?

options.text: Minimal classes wins because fewer classes are always better
The two never conflict
Expressiveness overrides both
Runs-all-the-tests wins because it is the higher-priority rule; minimality is applied last

questions.explanation: The four rules are in priority order: runs all the tests first, minimal classes/methods last, so testability outranks minimality (CleanCode p.80). Minimality does not win, the rules can conflict, and expressiveness ranks third. (CleanCode p.80)

questions.stem: Martin's chapter predates the 'SOLID' acronym yet covers all five letters. Which letter does the Clean Code deck supply that the principles deck only implies?

options.text: L (LSP) - found only in Clean Code
S (SRP) - stated explicitly on the Clean Code classes slide
D (DIP) - found only in Clean Code
O (OCP) - found only in Clean Code

questions.explanation: The principles deck covers OCP/LSP/DIP/ISP explicitly; SRP is stated on the Clean Code classes slide (CleanCode p.79), completing SOLID (guide Exam Focus). OCP, LSP, and DIP are in the principles deck. (CleanCode p.79)

questions.stem: A maintainer favors large packages to minimize how many packages a change touches; a reuser favors small packages. Which principles do they respectively champion?

options.text: Maintainer champions CRP; reuser champions CCP
Maintainer champions REP; reuser champions CCP
Maintainer champions CCP; reuser champions REP/CRP
Both champion ADP

questions.explanation: CCP (large packages, fewer touched by a change) serves maintainers; REP/CRP (small packages) serve reusers - the source of the cohesion tension (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18). The other pairings reverse the roles. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.18)

questions.stem: Why is Abstract Server arguably not a single named GoF pattern, unlike Adapter or Observer?

options.text: It was invented after the GoF book
It is identical to the Bridge pattern
GoF lists it as a creational pattern
GoF files the idea under programming-to-an-interface (OCP/DIP) rather than one named pattern

questions.explanation: Abstract Server is the structural realization of OCP/DIP; GoF would file it under programming-to-an-interface rather than as a single named pattern (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28). It is not creational, post-GoF, or identical to Bridge. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28)

questions.stem: What does the deck recommend doing with bad code rather than commenting it?

options.text: Rewrite it
Hide it behind an interface
Document it thoroughly
Add a TODO and move on

questions.explanation: 'Don't comment bad code, rewrite it!' (CleanCode p.34). Documenting, hiding, or deferring it does not fix the code. (CleanCode p.34)

questions.stem: Which of these is explicitly a BAD comment category in the deck?

options.text: Redundant comment
Legal comment
Informative comment
Explanation of intent

questions.explanation: Redundant comments restate the code and are a bad category (CleanCode p.39). Legal, informative, and intent comments are good categories. (CleanCode p.39)

questions.stem: What does 'vertical openness' between concepts use to signal a new concept?

options.text: A comment
A blank line
A semicolon
An indentation change

questions.explanation: Each blank line is a visual cue identifying a new concept (CleanCode p.53). Comments, semicolons, and indentation are not the cited cue. (CleanCode p.53)

questions.stem: What should class names be, according to the deck?

options.text: Verbs or verb phrases
Adjectives
Nouns or noun phrases
Adverbs

questions.explanation: Class names should be nouns or noun phrases (CleanCode p.18). Verbs are for methods; adjectives/adverbs are not used. (CleanCode p.18)

questions.stem: What should method names be, according to the deck?

options.text: Nouns or noun phrases
Adjectives
Single letters
Verbs or verb phrases

questions.explanation: Method names should be verbs or verb phrases (postPayment, save) (CleanCode p.19). Nouns are for classes; adjectives and single letters are not recommended. (CleanCode p.19)

questions.stem: What is the root cause that all four rotting-design symptoms share?

options.text: Slow hardware
Improper dependencies between modules
Inexperienced developers
Missing documentation

questions.explanation: All four symptoms stem from improper dependencies between modules (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4). Documentation, hardware, and experience are not the cited cause. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4)

questions.stem: Which principle did Bertrand Meyer originate, per the deck?

options.text: CRP (Common Reuse Principle)
LSP (Liskov Substitution Principle)
OCP (Open/Closed Principle)
ISP (Interface Segregation Principle)

questions.explanation: OCP originated with Bertrand Meyer (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4). LSP is Liskov's; ISP and CRP are Martin's. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.4)

questions.stem: Who coined the Liskov Substitution Principle?

options.text: Bertrand Meyer
Kent Beck
Robert Martin
Barbara Liskov

questions.explanation: LSP was coined by Barbara Liskov, related to Meyer's Design by Contract (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.8). Martin wrote the chapter; Beck wrote the simple-design rules. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.8)

questions.stem: Whose four 'rules of simple design' does the Emergence slide present?

options.text: Kent Beck's
Barbara Liskov's
Robert Martin's
Erich Gamma's

questions.explanation: The four rules of simple design are Kent Beck's (CleanCode p.80). Martin presents them; Liskov and Gamma are associated with other ideas. (CleanCode p.80)

questions.stem: Which five patterns does the DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns deck cover by name?

options.text: Abstract Server, Adapter, Observer, Bridge, Abstract Factory
Singleton, Factory Method, Strategy, Decorator, Composite
Visitor, Iterator, Memento, State, Proxy
Facade, Flyweight, Mediator, Command, Template Method

questions.explanation: The deck names Abstract Server, Adapter, Observer, Bridge, and Abstract Factory (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28-32). The other patterns exist in GoF but are not the five covered here. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.28)

questions.stem: What does it mean that an abstraction is a 'hinge point' in a design?

options.text: It is the point most likely to fail at runtime
The design can bend or be extended there without modifying the abstraction itself
It is where all concrete classes are instantiated
It is the slowest path in the call graph

questions.explanation: A hinge point is where the design flexes/extends without modifying the abstraction (OCP) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.13). It is not a failure point, instantiation site, or hotspot. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.13)

questions.stem: Why does the deck recommend grouping firstName/lastName/street into a class rather than prefixing each name?

options.text: Prefixes are faster to type
Classes use less memory than prefixed fields
A class provides meaningful context better than name prefixes
Prefixes are forbidden by the language

questions.explanation: Grouping into class Address gives meaningful context better than prefixing (addrFirstName) (CleanCode p.21). Typing speed, memory, and language rules are not the reason. (CleanCode p.21)

questions.stem: Why does Martin argue that 'concrete things change a lot, abstract things change less' supports DIP?

options.text: Abstract classes run faster than concrete ones
Concrete classes cannot be subclassed
Abstractions never need to change at all
Depending on stable abstractions insulates you from frequent concrete changes

questions.explanation: DIP rests on concretions being volatile and abstractions stable, so depending on abstractions insulates you from churn (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.13). Speed, subclassing, and 'never change' claims are false. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.13)

questions.stem: What is the purpose of horizontal openness, as in (-b - Math.sqrt(determinant)) / (2*a)?

options.text: Remove all spaces to maximize density
Use spaces to group and separate operands to clarify precedence
Align operators into vertical columns
Pad every line to the same width

questions.explanation: Horizontal openness uses spaces to group/separate so precedence reads clearly (CleanCode p.56). Padding to width, removing spaces, and column alignment are not the rule. (CleanCode p.56)

questions.stem: After replacing nested error-code checks with a try/catch, what is the next clean-code step for the try and catch bodies?

options.text: Wrap the whole thing in another try
Inline more logic into the try block
Extract each body into a one-line call to a well-named method
Add a finally block that returns a value

questions.explanation: Extract the try and catch bodies into named methods so each is a single call (CleanCode p.67). Inlining, value-returning finally, and nesting do not follow the rule. (CleanCode p.67)

questions.stem: Why does 'conceptual affinity' justify placing certain functions vertically close even without a direct call relationship?

options.text: It reduces the file's line count
It enforces single-exit control flow
The compiler optimizes adjacent functions
Related code should sit near related code so readers find it together

questions.explanation: Conceptual affinity keeps related code near related code (CleanCode p.55). It has no effect on compilation, line count, or control flow. (CleanCode p.55)

questions.stem: Where should instance variables be declared according to the vertical-distance rule?

options.text: At the top of the class
Inside the constructor only
Just before the first method that uses them
At the bottom of the class

questions.explanation: Instance variables belong at the top of the class; local variables go close to their use (CleanCode p.55). The other placements contradict the rule. (CleanCode p.55)

questions.stem: A test method contains six unrelated asserts covering different concepts. Which guideline does it violate?

options.text: Single concept per test (minimize asserts per concept, test one concept)
The First Law of TDD
The Boy Scout Rule
The newspaper metaphor

questions.explanation: Testing several concepts in one method violates 'single concept per test' (CleanCode p.76). The First Law, Boy Scout Rule, and newspaper metaphor address other things. (CleanCode p.76)

questions.stem: Adding a typeid(e)==typeid(Ellipse) check to a client to defend against a misbehaving Circle subtype is itself a violation of which principle?

options.text: DRY - the check is duplicated
OCP - the check must be revisited for every new subtype
ISP - the interface is too fat
REP - reuse and release are misaligned

questions.explanation: The defensive typeid check is an OCP violation, since it must be revisited for every new subtype - this is why LSP violations are latent OCP violations (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.11-12). DRY, ISP, and REP are different. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.12)

questions.stem: Which design change does Martin recommend FIRST when CommError accidentally depends on GUI, creating a cycle?

options.text: Delete the GUI package
Merge CommError and GUI into one package
Create a new package (e.g. extract MessageManager) to break the cycle
Add a build flag to ignore the cycle

questions.explanation: One way to break the cycle is to create a new package (extract MessageManager); the other is DIP+ISP inversion (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.20-22). Deleting, merging, or ignoring does not properly break it. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.20)

questions.stem: In the Observer pattern, how does a Meter get registered to receive updates from a Sensor's Subject?

options.text: The Meter polls the Sensor on a fixed timer
The Sensor constructs the Meter directly
A factory binds them at startup
The Subject exposes Register(Observer*) which adds the Meter to its observer list

questions.explanation: The Subject holds a list of Observers populated via Register(Observer*) (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30). Polling, direct construction, and factory binding are not the registration mechanism. (DesignPrinciplesAndPatterns p.30)
